solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell cycled 200 times at -5°C and a cell cycled 200 times at 25°C both reach approximately 80% SOH. During accelerating rate calorimetry, which statements correctly describe differences between these two cells? Select all that apply.
options: A The -5°C-cycled cell enters self-heating at a lower onset temperature (~82°C vs ~102°C for fresh cells).
B The 25°C-cycled cell shows substantially lower activation energy for thermal runaway than the fresh cell.
C The -5°C-cycled cell's earlier self-heating onset is mechanistically linked to reduced activation energy for thermal runaway.
D Because both cells share ~80% SOH, their thermal runaway onset temperatures and delay times are equivalent.
E EIS of the -5°C-cycled cell shows minimal change in ohmic and charge-transfer resistance, consistent with lithium plating as the dominant aging mechanism.
F The -5°C-cycled cell ejects more mass during thermal runaway than the fresh cell due to accumulated lithium deposits.
G SOH alone is insufficient to characterize thermal runaway risk when aging conditions differ.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states onset temperature drops from ~102°C to ~82°C for -5°C-cycled cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 25°C-cycled cells have activation energy nearly identical to fresh cells, not substantially lower.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly links lower activation energy in low-temperature-aged cells to earlier self-heating onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly refutes this: similar SOH does not imply equivalent TR onset or delay time.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: EIS showing minimal resistance change with no dual semicircles indicates lithium plating, not SEI thickening.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states fresh cells show higher mass loss; aged cells lose less material due to capacity and active material degradation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states SOH is insufficient to characterize TR risk when aging pathways differ.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing aging-pathway-dependent TR risk from SOH-equivalent comparisons
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings apply to cells at ~80% SOH with differing cycling temperature histories; SOH equivalence does not imply TR equivalence
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing low-temperature aging effects to all aging modes
Assuming lithium plating universally dominates at any sub-zero temperature

solver_view.stem: A battery safety engineer compares four cell groups: fresh cells, cells cycled at 25°C, cells cycled at -5°C, and cells from a used consumer device (dynamic cycling with calendar aging), all at similar SOH (~80%). During thermal abuse testing, voltage drops occur at relatively lower temperatures only in the consumer-device cells. Which conclusions are supported by this observation?
options: A Consumer-device cells are more sensitive to internal short circuit triggers than the other aged groups.
B The earlier voltage drop in consumer-device cells implies they also have the lowest self-heating onset temperature among all groups.
C Dynamic cycling combined with calendar aging produces a distinct aging signature not captured by constant-temperature lab cycling alone.
D The -5°C-cycled cells will show voltage drops at higher temperatures than the consumer-device cells because lithium plating raises internal resistance.
E Voltage drop temperature alone is sufficient to rank all four groups by thermal runaway severity.
F The consumer-device result demonstrates that real-world aging profiles can produce thermal behavior not predicted by single-variable lab aging protocols.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states consumer-device cells are more sensitive to internal short circuit triggers based on early voltage drop.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies lowest self-heating onset in -5°C-cycled cells; no evidence links consumer-device cells to lowest onset temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Consumer-device aging combines dynamic cycling and calendar aging, producing distinct voltage-drop behavior not seen in isothermal lab cycling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports ranking -5°C cells above consumer-device cells on voltage-drop temperature; this conflates resistance increase with short-circuit sensitivity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not support voltage drop temperature as a sufficient universal ranking metric for TR severity across all conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Consumer-device result shows real-world multi-variable aging produces behavior distinct from controlled single-variable lab protocols.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing internal short circuit sensitivity from self-heating onset; evaluating scope of lab aging protocols
analysis.primary_boundary: Consumer-device finding is specific to dynamic+calendar aging; cannot be generalized to all real-world cells
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution between short-circuit sensitivity and self-heating onset
regime_transfer_error conflating resistance with short-circuit trigger
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming consumer-device cells have lowest TR onset among all groups
Using voltage drop temperature as universal TR severity metric

solver_view.prompt: A cell aged by low-temperature cycling at -5°C and a cell aged by cycling at 25°C both reach 80% SOH. An engineer argues that because their capacities are identical, their thermal runaway risks are equivalent and standard SOH monitoring is sufficient for safety screening. Using the mechanistic evidence available, identify the two key reasons this argument is flawed and explain the causal chain connecting low-temperature aging to elevated thermal runaway risk.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The argument fails for two reasons. First, SOH equivalence does not imply equivalent TR risk: despite matched ~80% SOH, -5°C-cycled cells show a ~20°C lower self-heating onset temperature (~82°C vs ~102°C) and shorter TR delay time, whereas 25°C-cycled cells retain kinetics nearly identical to fresh cells. Second, the dominant aging mechanism differs: low-temperature cycling causes lithium plating rather than SEI thickening, evidenced by minimal change in ohmic and charge-transfer resistance with no dual semicircles in EIS. The causal chain is: lithium plating deposits reactive metallic lithium → reduces activation energy for exothermic reactions → lowers self-heating onset temperature → accelerates entry into thermal runaway. SOH captures capacity loss but not this kinetic hazard.
must_include: SOH alone is insufficient to characterize TR risk
self-heating onset decreases from ~102°C to ~82°C for -5°C-cycled cells
lithium plating is the dominant aging mechanism at -5°C
EIS shows minimal resistance change, not SEI thickening
lower activation energy links lithium plating to earlier self-heating onset
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming lithium plating universally dominates below 0°C for all chemistries
Asserting consumer-device cells share the same mechanism
Generalizing that all aged cells have lower TR onset than fresh cells

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain from aging mechanism to TR kinetics, and limitation of SOH as a safety metric
analysis.primary_boundary: Findings are specific to the -5°C cycling condition; 25°C-cycled cells do not show the same kinetic shift
intended_error_modes: Assuming SOH equivalence implies TR equivalence
Attributing low-temperature aging effect to SEI thickening instead of lithium plating
Overgeneralizing activation energy reduction to all aging modes

solver_view.stem: A pouch lithium-ion battery is overcharged adiabatically at 1/3C to 1C. Thermal runaway temperature (TTR) remains near 113 °C and SOCTR decreases only slightly (1.674 to 1.652). DSC of overcharged cathodes shows no exothermic activity below 250 °C. Anode heat generation rises from 1592 J/g at SOC 1.00 to 2553 J/g at SOC 1.85, with lithium metal deposition beginning above SOC 1.25. Which statements are supported by these observations?
options: A. Cathode decomposition is the primary driver of heat generation before thermal runaway under these overcharge conditions.
B. Deposited lithium metal on the anode is more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperature and accelerates battery temperature rise.
C. Charging rate between 1/3C and 1C is a dominant factor controlling the SOC at which thermal runaway occurs.
D. Anode-side reactions become increasingly significant as overcharge progresses beyond SOC 1.25.
E. The minor variation in SOCTR across charging rates implies that heat accumulation rate, not SOC, primarily governs thermal runaway onset under adiabatic conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathodes show no exothermic behavior below 250 °C, well above the observed TTR < 150 °C; cathode contribution is explicitly ruled out.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Anode heat generation exceeds 1.5× initial value with lithium deposition; evidence directly states deposited lithium accelerates temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SOCTR changes only from 1.674 to 1.652 across the tested rate range; evidence explicitly labels charging current influence as minor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lithium deposition begins at SOC 1.25 and anode heat generation grows substantially thereafter, supporting increasing anode-side significance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence notes TTR is stable and SOCTR varies little, but does not establish heat accumulation rate as the governing variable over SOC; this is an unsupported causal overclaim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing anode vs. cathode contributions to pre-TR heat generation and evaluating charging rate influence on SOCTR
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR < 150 °C; cathode exotherms only above 250 °C; charging rate range 1/3C–1C
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing cathode inactivity below 250 °C to all chemistries or temperatures
Extending minor SOCTR variation to claim rate is irrelevant at all C-rates

solver_view.stem: Two pouch battery configurations are tested adiabatically: Configuration A (no restraining plate, no pouch cuts) reaches TTR at 113.1 °C and SOCTR 1.670 via pouch rupture and gas ejection igniting flammable gases. Configuration C (restraining plate plus pouch cuts) reaches TTR at 140.9 °C and SOCTR 1.738 via separator melting at ~141 °C causing internal short circuit. Which statements correctly characterize the mechanism difference and its implications?
options: A. Configuration C delays TR by preventing gas pressure buildup, allowing the battery to reach the PE separator melting point before runaway.
B. In configuration A, the primary ignition source is ohmic heat from internal short circuit caused by separator shrinkage.
C. The restraining plate in configuration C suppresses pouch swelling, shifting the TR initiation mechanism from external gas ignition to separator-melt-induced internal short circuit.
D. Configuration C's higher SOCTR relative to A is consistent with the restraining plate enabling greater lithium deposition before TR initiates.
E. Voltage and resistance profiles are significantly altered by the restraining plate, explaining the improved overcharge performance in configuration C.
F. The TR initiation temperature in configuration C coincides with the DSC-measured PE melting point, implicating separator integrity as the limiting factor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cuts allow gas venting without pouch rupture-driven ignition; battery reaches ~141 °C PE melting point before TR, consistent with evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: In configuration A, TR is triggered by sparks igniting ejected flammable gas, not by internal short circuit from separator shrinkage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly contrasts pouch-rupture/gas-ignition in A with separator-melt internal short circuit in C, attributing the shift to the restraining plate configuration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher SOCTR is mechanistically linked to delayed TR initiation, not specifically to greater lithium deposition; this causal attribution is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states voltage and resistance profiles remain almost unchanged with the restraining plate, contradicting this option.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TR in configuration C occurs at ~141 °C, matching DSC-measured PE melting temperature; separator integrity is directly identified as the limiting factor.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism-level comparison of TR initiation pathways across configurations and the role of separator melting vs. gas ignition
analysis.primary_boundary: Adiabatic condition; configurations A and C only; TTR < 150 °C
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying configuration C findings to non-adiabatic conditions without noting SOCTR difference
Generalizing PE melting trigger to all separator chemistries

solver_view.stem: A battery with configuration C (restraining plate, pouch cuts) is tested under adiabatic conditions (SOCTR = 1.738, TTR = 140.9 °C) and then under non-adiabatic conditions (SOCTR = 1.854). Heat dissipation is improved in the non-adiabatic case. Lithium deposition begins above SOC 1.25. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Improved heat dissipation reduces heat accumulation, allowing the battery to sustain higher SOC before reaching the thermal runaway threshold.
B. The SOCTR increase from 1.738 to 1.854 under non-adiabatic conditions demonstrates that thermal management can fully prevent thermal runaway in overcharge scenarios.
C. Because lithium deposition begins at SOC 1.25, the non-adiabatic battery at SOCTR 1.854 has accumulated substantial deposited lithium before TR onset.
D. Non-adiabatic conditions improve overcharge performance primarily by suppressing lithium deposition on the anode.

E. The SOCTR improvement under non-adiabatic versus adiabatic conditions is especially pronounced above SOC 1.6, consistent with evidence that heat dissipation matters more when heat generation rates are elevated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states heat dissipation lowers heat accumulation within the battery, enabling higher SOCTR before TR threshold is reached.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TR still occurs at SOCTR 1.854; evidence shows improvement, not prevention. Claiming full prevention is an unsupported boundary overreach.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Deposition starts at SOC 1.25; SOCTR 1.854 >> 1.25, so substantial lithium deposition has occurred before TR onset under non-adiabatic conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes improvement to reduced heat accumulation, not to suppression of lithium deposition; this is a mechanism substitution error.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly notes the significant SOCTR increase especially when SOC exceeds 1.6, directly supporting this conditional claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Effect of heat dissipation on overcharge tolerance and interaction with lithium deposition threshold
analysis.primary_boundary: Configuration C only; SOC deposition threshold 1.25; non-adiabatic vs. adiabatic comparison
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming non-adiabatic conditions eliminate TR
Attributing SOCTR improvement to electrochemical rather than thermal mechanism

solver_view.prompt: A pouch lithium-ion battery is overcharged adiabatically. Two configurations are compared: Configuration A (no restraining plate, no pouch cuts) undergoes thermal runaway at TTR = 113.1 °C and SOCTR = 1.670. Configuration C (restraining plate plus pouch cuts) undergoes thermal runaway at TTR = 140.9 °C and SOCTR = 1.738. DSC of overcharged cathodes shows no exothermic activity below 250 °C. Lithium metal deposits on the anode above SOC 1.25, and anode heat generation rises to over 1.5× its initial value by SOC 1.85. Explain the dominant heat generation mechanism before TR in these overcharge tests and why cathode decomposition can be excluded as a significant contributor under these conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Before thermal runaway, the dominant heat source is the anode, not the cathode. Overcharged cathodes show no measurable exothermic activity until approximately 250 °C, which is well above the observed TTR of less than 150 °C in all tests; cathode decomposition therefore cannot contribute meaningfully to heat generation before TR occurs. On the anode side, lithium metal begins depositing once SOC exceeds 1.25. Deposited lithium is more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperatures, and anode heat generation increases to more than 1.5 times its initial value as SOC rises toward 1.85. This progressive increase in anode reactivity is the primary mechanism accelerating battery temperature rise during overcharge, ultimately driving the system to the TR threshold.
must_include: Cathode exotherms only above ~250 °C, above the observed TTR < 150 °C
Cathode decomposition excluded as significant pre-TR heat source
Lithium metal deposition on anode begins above SOC 1.25
Anode heat generation increases to more than 1.5× initial value
Deposited lithium more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperature
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cathode is inactive at all temperatures or in all chemistries
Stating anode reactions alone trigger TR without acknowledging the thermal threshold condition
Generalizing findings beyond the tested adiabatic overcharge conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal reasoning linking SOC-dependent lithium deposition to anode heat generation increase, while excluding cathode contribution via temperature boundary argument
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR < 150 °C; cathode exotherm onset ~250 °C; lithium deposition threshold SOC 1.25; adiabatic overcharge only

solver_view.stem: A 3×3 battery pack with a center cell heater is tested at fixed heating power. Spacing is increased from 2 mm to 4 mm, then from 4 mm to 6 mm. Which of the following statements are consistent with the observed behavior?
options: A The delay in thermal runaway increases by 150 s when spacing goes from 2 mm to 4 mm.
B The delay increment is larger when spacing increases from 4 mm to 6 mm than from 2 mm to 4 mm.
C Increasing spacing from 4 mm to 6 mm produces a 30 K drop in maximum pack temperature.
D Each equal increment in spacing produces an equal additional delay in thermal runaway onset.
E The 50 s smaller delay increment from 4–6 mm compared to 2–4 mm indicates diminishing returns in spacing benefit.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states 150 s delay gained by increasing spacing from 2 mm to 4 mm.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The delay increment from 4–6 mm is 50 s smaller than the 2–4 mm increment, so it is lesser, not larger.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states a 30 K drop in maximum temperature when spacing increases from 4 mm to 6 mm.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states the reduction is non-uniform with increasing spacing, contradicting equal increments.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A smaller delay increment at larger spacing directly implies diminishing returns, consistent with non-uniform reduction evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-uniform spacing effect on thermal runaway delay and peak temperature in center-heated pack
analysis.primary_boundary: Center cell heating configuration; spacing range 2–6 mm only
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending diminishing-returns trend beyond 6 mm spacing
Applying center-cell results to side-cell heating configuration

solver_view.stem: In a 3×3 NCA 18650 pack at 7000 W/m², spacing is 6 mm and coolant air flows from row 1 toward row 3. The center cell of row 1 is the heated trigger cell. Which statements correctly describe pack-level thermal runaway propagation?
options: A Row 3 (downstream) cells fail before row 1 cells because heated air accumulates toward the outlet.
B The entire 9-cell pack enters thermal runaway within 25–30 s after the first cell triggers.
C Row 1 cells fail first because they are closest to the trigger and upstream cooling is least effective there.
D Increasing heating power from 7000 W/m² to 10,000 W/m² raises the maximum pack temperature by 10 K.
E The 25–30 s full-pack propagation time applies specifically to 6 mm spacing at 7000 W/m².
F Downstream row failure before upstream row is driven by coolant flow direction, not proximity to trigger.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states last row fails quickly because air flows from first to last row, carrying heat downstream.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states entire 9-cell pack enters thermal runaway within 25–30 s at these conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Upstream row 1 is cooled by incoming fresh air; downstream row fails first, opposite of this claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 7000 W/m² pack reaches 10 K higher maximum temperature than 10,000 W/m², reversing the direction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 25–30 s observation is tied to 6 mm spacing and 7000 W/m² conditions and cannot be generalized freely.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence gives coolant flow direction as the causal explanation for downstream row failing first, not proximity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Coolant-flow-driven propagation direction and full-pack runaway timeline
analysis.primary_boundary: 6 mm spacing, 7000 W/m², 3×3 pack, center cell trigger
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 25–30 s propagation time to other spacings or powers
Assuming downstream failure pattern holds for all flow configurations

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway of a single 18650 NCA cell, four exothermic reactions release heat. Rank-based reasoning is applied to assess which reaction dominates and how electrolyte breakdown compares. Which statements are supported?
options: A Negative solvent reaction is the dominant heat source, releasing approximately 1984 W.
B Internal short circuit releases more heat than positive solvent decomposition during thermal runaway.
C Electrolyte breakdown is the dominant heat-release mechanism because it initiates the cascade.
D Negative solvent reaction releases roughly 13 times more heat than electrolyte breakdown.
E Positive solvent decomposition releases more heat than electrolyte breakdown but less than internal short circuit.
F The four reactions together confirm that cathode-side chemistry dominates total heat release.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states negative solvent reaction releases 1984 W, the highest among all four reactions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Internal short circuit releases 1157 W vs. positive solvent decomposition 479 W, so ISC > PSD.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte breakdown releases only 148 W, the lowest of the four; initiating role is not supported by heat magnitude.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 1984 W ÷ 148 W ≈ 13.4; evidence explicitly states this 13× ratio.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 479 W (PSD) > 148 W (electrolyte) and 479 W < 1157 W (ISC), consistent with evidence values.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Negative solvent reaction is anode-side chemistry; claiming cathode-side dominance contradicts the evidence ranking.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Relative heat contribution of four TR reactions and identification of dominant mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: Single 18650 NCA cell thermal runaway heat release values
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying NCA heat release hierarchy to other chemistries
Inferring initiation sequence from heat magnitude alone

solver_view.prompt: A 18650 NCA cell is externally heated at 5000 W/m². Two heating positions are compared: center 10 mm and vertical 10 mm. Using the reported safety order and temperature data, explain mechanistically why center 10 mm heating is both the least safe position and produces the highest maximum temperature (60–70 K above other positions). Also state whether the safety ranking from this single-cell experiment can be directly applied to predict heating-position safety in a multi-cell pack, and justify your boundary judgment.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Center 10 mm heating targets the electrochemically active mid-section of the cell, where electrode reactions and heat-generating TR mechanisms (negative solvent reaction, ISC) are concentrated. Localized heating at this region raises local temperature faster, triggering TR reactions earlier and driving maximum temperatures 60–70 K higher than other positions. Vertical 10 mm heating distributes flux along the cell axis, reducing peak local temperature and delaying TR onset, making it the safest position. The single-cell safety ranking cannot be directly transferred to a multi-cell pack: in a pack, coolant flow direction, inter-cell spacing, and neighbor-cell heat coupling alter which position drives propagation fastest. The evidence on pack behavior (flow-driven downstream failure, spacing effects) involves different governing variables not present in single-cell tests.
must_include: center 10 mm produces 60–70 K higher maximum temperature than other positions
vertical 10 mm is the safest single-cell heating position
single-cell ranking cannot be directly applied to pack-level safety without accounting for coolant flow and spacing effects
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming center heating is universally most dangerous in all pack geometries
Asserting the 60–70 K differential persists at heating powers other than 5000 W/m²
Inferring TR initiation sequence from heating position alone without chemistry context

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism linking heating position to local temperature elevation and TR timing, plus boundary of single-cell-to-pack generalization
analysis.primary_boundary: 5000 W/m² heating, single 18650 NCA cell; pack results involve additional variables
intended_error_modes: scale transfer overclaim from single cell to pack
boundary overreach applying single-cell safety order universally
causal overclaim attributing temperature differential solely to heating area size

solver_view.stem: A prismatic lithium-ion cell undergoes 0.5C overcharge under uniaxial constraint. The expansion force peaks at ~13,919 N before venting. At the moment the safety valve opens, strain hardening contributes +64.67 MPa, strain rate hardening contributes +22.15 MPa, and thermal softening reduces yield strength by 18.41 MPa. Which statements correctly characterize the mechanical state of the casing at safety valve activation?
options: A. The net yield strength change is positive, meaning the casing is mechanically stronger than its undeformed baseline at this moment.
B. Thermal softening is the dominant factor controlling yield strength at safety valve activation, exceeding combined strengthening by more than 20%.
C. Combined strengthening effects exceed thermal softening by more than 300%, indicating deformation-driven hardening dominates over temperature-driven weakening at this stage.
D. Battery surface temperature has already exceeded 200°C before the expansion force peak, driving the observed high expansion force.
E. The large expansion force before venting can occur while surface temperature remains below 50°C, indicating force is not a reliable proxy for surface thermal hazard at this stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Net change = +64.67 +22.15 -18.41 = +68.41 MPa > 0, so yield strength is above baseline.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal softening (18.41 MPa) is far smaller than combined strengthening (86.82 MPa); direction of dominance is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states total strengthening is 371.59% greater than thermal softening at safety valve activation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states surface temperature remained below 50°C before the expansion force peak, contradicting >200°C claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Force peaked at ~13,919 N while surface temperature stayed below 50°C, decoupling force magnitude from surface thermal state.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanical state at safety valve activation: sign and dominance of yield strength contributors, and decoupling of expansion force from surface temperature.
analysis.primary_boundary: Conditions specific to safety valve activation moment during 0.5C overcharge; not generalizable to post-TR state.
disallowed_extrapolations: Post-TR softening dominance cannot be applied to pre-vent state.
High expansion force does not imply high surface temperature.

solver_view.stem: After thermal runaway in a 0.5C overcharge test, the casing yield strength reaches its minimum. At this point: strain hardening contributes +78.93 MPa, strain rate hardening contributes +25.34 MPa, and thermal softening reduces yield strength by 151.35 MPa. The cell is under uniaxial constraint. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Post-TR thermal softening exceeds combined strengthening, reversing the mechanical dominance relationship observed at safety valve activation.
B. The weakening effect exceeds combined strengthening by approximately 45%, meaning the casing is at its structurally weakest relative state after TR.
C. Because strain hardening increased from 64.67 MPa to 78.93 MPa after TR, the casing is net stronger post-TR than at safety valve activation.
D. Under uniaxial constraint post-TR, maximum plastic strain concentrates at the top and bottom plate corners rather than the side walls.
E. The post-TR reversal of mechanical dominance implies that surface temperature also reverses its trend and drops below 50°C after TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Post-TR softening (151.35 MPa) exceeds combined strengthening (104.27 MPa), reversing the pre-vent dominance relationship.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states weakening exceeds combined strengthening by 45.15% at minimum yield strength after TR.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Net yield strength change post-TR = +78.93+25.34-151.35 = -47.08 MPa < 0; casing is net weaker despite higher strain hardening.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Under uniaxial constraint, side-wall suppression forces top/bottom plates to bulge, concentrating plastic strain at corner discontinuities.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links post-TR mechanical reversal to a temperature drop; TR produces ~475°C, not sub-50°C conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Post-TR mechanical dominance reversal and deformation localization under uniaxial constraint.
analysis.primary_boundary: Post-TR minimum yield strength state; uniaxial constraint geometry.
disallowed_extrapolations: Increased strain hardening magnitude does not imply net strengthening when softening is larger.
Mechanical reversal does not imply thermal reversal.

solver_view.stem: An engineer redesigns a prismatic cell casing tested under 0.5C overcharge with uniaxial constraint. Two proposals are compared against a 1.5 mm uniform baseline: (P1) increase top and bottom plate thickness to 3.0 mm, keeping side walls at 1.5 mm; (P2) increase only side-wall thickness, keeping top/bottom at 1.5 mm. The goal is to reduce plastic strain in the critical corner region. Which statements are supported by the evidence?
options: A. P1 reduces corner-region PEEQ by 40% and top-surface PEEQ by 51.5%, making it effective for corner protection.
B. P2 produces nearly identical PEEQ across all three casing regions, offering negligible corner protection.
C. P2 is preferable because thicker side walls directly resist the bulging deformation mode under uniaxial constraint.
D. P1 and P2 provide equivalent corner protection because both increase total casing material volume.
E. The effectiveness of P1 over P2 is mechanistically consistent with bending stiffness scaling as the cube of thickness, making plate thickness disproportionately influential.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence reports exactly 40.0% corner PEEQ reduction and 51.5% top-surface PEEQ reduction for 1.5→3.0 mm top plate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states side-wall thickening yields nearly identical PEEQ in all three regions, providing negligible corner protection.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Under uniaxial constraint, side walls are suppressed; bulging occurs at top/bottom plates, so side-wall thickening does not address the dominant deformation mode.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Equivalent material volume does not imply equivalent protection; location and bending stiffness scaling determine effectiveness.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly cites cubic thickness scaling of bending stiffness as the mechanistic reason top/bottom plate thickening outperforms side-wall thickening.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative effectiveness of plate vs. side-wall thickening for corner protection under uniaxial constraint, linked to bending stiffness scaling.
analysis.primary_boundary: 0.5C overcharge, uniaxial constraint, specific thickness range 1.5–3.0 mm top plate.
disallowed_extrapolations: Results cannot be universalized to all constraint geometries or chemistries.
Volume equivalence does not imply protection equivalence.

solver_view.prompt: During a 0.5C overcharge test of a prismatic lithium-ion cell under uniaxial constraint, two distinct mechanical regimes are observed: one at safety valve activation and one at the post-thermal-runaway yield strength minimum. Describe how the balance between strengthening mechanisms (strain hardening, strain rate hardening) and thermal softening shifts between these two stages, and explain what structural consequence this shift has for the casing, referencing the constraint geometry.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At safety valve activation, combined strengthening (strain hardening +64.67 MPa, strain rate hardening +22.15 MPa) exceeds thermal softening (−18.41 MPa) by 371.59%, so the casing yield strength is net positive and deformation-hardening dominates. After thermal runaway, thermal softening surges to −151.35 MPa while strengthening grows only modestly (+78.93 and +25.34 MPa), causing weakening to exceed combined strengthening by 45.15%—a full reversal. Under uniaxial constraint, side-wall deformation is suppressed throughout, forcing top and bottom plates to bulge outward and concentrating maximum plastic strain at corner discontinuities. The post-TR reversal means the casing reaches its structurally weakest state precisely when deformation demand at corners is highest, elevating fracture risk at those locations.
must_include: strengthening exceeds softening at safety valve activation (371.59% or equivalent quantitative relationship)
softening exceeds strengthening after TR (45.15% or equivalent reversal)
uniaxial constraint suppresses side walls, forcing top/bottom plate bulging
corner concentration of plastic strain
disallowed_extrapolations: Do not claim the reversal occurs at a specific temperature without evidence.
Do not generalize the quantitative ratios to other C-rates or chemistries.
Do not claim casing fracture is confirmed; only that risk is elevated.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Two-stage mechanical regime shift (hardening-dominant to softening-dominant) and its structural consequence under uniaxial constraint geometry.
analysis.primary_boundary: 0.5C overcharge, uniaxial constraint, prismatic cell; quantitative ratios apply only to reported test conditions.

solver_view.stem: NCM811|Gr pouch cells are charged to 4.2 V using either a base electrolyte (BE) or a high-safety electrolyte (HSE). Thermal runaway is induced by external heating. The BE cell triggers thermal runaway at T2 = 223.6 °C with a peak temperature T3 = 715.4 °C. The HSE cell shows T2 = 248.0 °C and T3 = 537.7 °C. The incubation time extends from 8.03 × 10⁴ s (BE) to 9.25 × 10⁴ s (HSE). Which of the following conclusions are supported by these observations?
options: A. HSE raises the thermal runaway trigger temperature by approximately 24 °C relative to BE at 4.2 V.
B. HSE eliminates thermal runaway entirely in NCM811|Gr cells at 4.2 V.
C. HSE reduces the maximum temperature reached during thermal runaway by approximately 178 °C relative to BE at 4.2 V.
D. The extended incubation time with HSE indicates postponement of thermal decomposition onset.
E. HSE lowers T2 by suppressing cathode oxidation before the trigger point, as shown by the incubation data alone.
F. The reduction in T3 with HSE is consistent with reduced heat release during the runaway event.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T2 increases from 223.6 °C to 248.0 °C, a difference of 24.4 °C, directly supported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows TR still occurs with HSE at 4.2 V; only temperatures are shifted, not TR eliminated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T3 drops from 715.4 °C to 537.7 °C, a reduction of 177.7 °C, directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Longer incubation time is explicitly described as demonstrating postponement of thermal decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Incubation time data alone cannot isolate cathode oxidation suppression; causal mechanism is overclaimed from timing data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower T3 is consistent with reduced heat release; radical-quenching mechanism supports this inference.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing supported quantitative comparisons from overclaimed mechanistic or eliminative conclusions
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are confined to 4.2 V, NCM811|Gr chemistry; HSE delays/reduces but does not eliminate TR
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing TR elimination to other voltages or chemistries
Attributing T2 shift solely to cathode effects from incubation data

solver_view.stem: NCM811|Gr pouch cells with base electrolyte (BE) show thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 = 223.6 °C at 4.2 V and T2 = 194.6 °C at 4.5 V (a drop of 29 °C). Cells with HSE show T2 = 248.0 °C at 4.2 V and T2 = 245.0 °C at 4.5 V. A ceramic-coated separator is present but thermal runaway still occurs. Which statements are consistent with these findings?
options: A. Increasing cut-off voltage from 4.2 V to 4.5 V significantly destabilizes BE-based cells, lowering T2 by ~29 °C.
B. HSE-based cells show comparable voltage sensitivity to BE-based cells when cut-off voltage increases from 4.2 V to 4.5 V.
C. The ceramic-coated separator prevents thermal runaway by blocking all chemical species transport between electrodes.
D. HSE confers near-voltage-insensitive T2 behavior between 4.2 V and 4.5 V, with T2 changing by only ~3 °C.
E. Reductive gas migration through separator pores can sustain thermal runaway even when direct electrode contact is blocked.
F. The 29 °C drop in T2 for BE cells at 4.5 V implies that BE cells are safer at higher voltages due to earlier venting.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T2 for BE drops from 223.6 °C to 194.6 °C across the voltage range, a 29 °C decrease, directly evidenced.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: BE drops 29 °C while HSE drops only 3 °C; their voltage sensitivities are markedly different, not comparable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states TR still occurs because reactive species migrate through separator pores despite ceramic coating.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: HSE T2 changes only 3 °C (248.0 to 245.0 °C) across the voltage range, supporting near-voltage-insensitive behavior.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states reductive/oxide species migrate through separator holes, sustaining TR despite blocked direct contact.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lower T2 means TR triggers at a lower temperature, indicating greater hazard, not improved safety; causal direction reversed.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Voltage sensitivity contrast between BE and HSE, and limitations of physical separator barriers
analysis.primary_boundary: Voltage range 4.2–4.5 V, NCM811|Gr chemistry; ceramic separator present but insufficient alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming ceramic separator eliminates TR
Equating lower T2 with improved safety

solver_view.stem: A 9-cell NCM811|Gr battery module simulation compares BE and HSE cells. BE cells show thermal runaway propagation at 29 s per cell with a module peak temperature of 730.6 °C. HSE cells self-quench after three cells trigger thermal runaway, with a module peak temperature of 202.7 °C. HSE has a self-extinguishing time (SET) of zero versus 29.3 s g⁻¹ for BE. PO• radicals from PFPN pyrolysis capture H• and OH• radicals. Which statements are supported?
options: A. In the HSE module, thermal runaway propagation arrests after three cells, preventing whole-module failure.
B. The non-flammable character of HSE, evidenced by SET = 0, contributes mechanistically to limiting module-level propagation.
C. PO• radical scavenging of H• and OH• impedes combustion chain reactions, consistent with reduced heat release at module scale.
D. HSE guarantees zero thermal runaway events at module level regardless of cell count or abuse severity.
E. The 29 s per cell propagation rate in BE modules implies that module failure is complete within approximately 4 minutes for a 9-cell pack.
F. Radical quenching by PO• is the sole mechanism responsible for the module-level temperature reduction observed with HSE.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states only three cells trigger TR and the module self-quenches, preventing full propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SET = 0 indicates non-flammable electrolyte; non-flammability mechanistically limits heat feeding propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PO• captures H• and OH•, impeding chain reactions and reducing heat release, consistent with lower module peak temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Three cells still trigger TR in the HSE module; zero TR events is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 9 cells × 29 s per cell = 232 s ≈ 3.9 minutes; arithmetic is consistent with the stated propagation rate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Non-flammability (SET = 0) and elevated T2 also contribute; attributing reduction solely to PO• scavenging is an unsupported single-cause claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Module-level propagation arrest mechanisms and admissibility of single-cause claims
analysis.primary_boundary: 9-cell simulation under defined abuse; HSE limits but does not eliminate TR at cell level
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming HSE prevents all TR events
Attributing module temperature reduction to a single mechanism

solver_view.prompt: An engineer proposes two safety strategies for NCM811|Gr pouch cells operated at up to 4.5 V: (1) replacing the standard electrolyte with a high-safety electrolyte (HSE) containing fluorine- and phosphorus-based additives, and (2) applying a ceramic coating to the separator to block electrode cross-contact. Using the evidence available, evaluate the effectiveness and limitations of each strategy with respect to thermal runaway prevention, and explain why neither strategy alone is sufficient to fully prevent thermal runaway under high-voltage conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Strategy 1 (HSE): HSE raises the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 by ~24 °C at 4.2 V and keeps T2 nearly unchanged (~3 °C drop) when voltage increases to 4.5 V, unlike base electrolyte which drops 29 °C. HSE reduces peak temperature T3 by ~178 °C and extends incubation time. Its non-flammable character (SET = 0) and PO•/H• radical scavenging reduce heat release. However, thermal runaway still occurs with HSE; it is delayed and reduced, not eliminated. Strategy 2 (ceramic separator): Blocking direct electrode contact does not prevent TR because reductive gas species migrate through separator pores to attack the cathode, sustaining chemical crosstalk. Physical barrier alone is therefore insufficient. Combined limitation: HSE addresses heat generation and flame propagation but not all ignition pathways; the ceramic separator addresses contact but not gas-phase transport. Neither alone eliminates TR.
must_include: HSE raises T2 and reduces T3 but does not eliminate thermal runaway
HSE T2 is nearly voltage-insensitive between 4.2 V and 4.5 V unlike base electrolyte
Ceramic separator fails because reactive species migrate through pores
HSE non-flammability (SET = 0) linked to radical scavenging mechanism
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming HSE fully prevents thermal runaway at any voltage
Claiming ceramic separator provides any protection against gas-phase chemical crossover
Generalizing findings to chemistries other than NCM811|Gr

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative evaluation of two independent safety strategies and their mechanistic limitations under high-voltage NCM811|Gr conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: NCM811|Gr, 4.2–4.5 V; HSE reduces but does not eliminate TR; ceramic separator insufficient against gas migration
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution attributing separator failure to wrong cause
scale_transfer overclaiming cell-level results to all module conditions

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell is cycled 400 times at 20°C until SOH reaches 70%. Post-mortem analysis confirms metallic lithium deposits on the anode. The cell is then heated from ambient temperature. Which statements correctly describe the expected thermal behavior of this aged cell compared to a fresh fully charged cell of the same chemistry?
options: A. The onset temperature for exothermic reactions is lower in the aged cell due to metallic lithium deposits reacting at reduced temperatures.
B. The thermal runaway temperature of the aged cell is substantially lower than 214°C because accumulated anode degradation dominates cathode decomposition onset.
C. The correlation between onset temperature and SOC is less pronounced in the aged cell than in fresh cells.
D. The thermal runaway temperature of the aged cell falls within approximately 214–221°C, similar to fresh fully charged cells.
E. The anode deposits shift the dominant mechanism controlling thermal runaway away from cathode decomposition and electrolyte oxidation.
F. The thick decomposition layer on the anode causes the thermal runaway temperature to exceed 300°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Metallic lithium in the decomposition layer causes earlier onset of exothermic reactions at lower temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TTR is dominated by cathode decomposition; aged cells still show TTR in 214–221°C range, not substantially lower.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Aged 20°C cells show less pronounced SOC-onset correlation due to metallic lithium deposits acting as SOC-independent reactants.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows TTR for fresh and aged fully charged cells remains in the narrow 214–221°C range regardless of cycling history.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal runaway remains dominated by cathode reactivity with electrolyte; anode only governs onset, not TTR mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TTR >300°C applies only to fully discharged (SOC 0%) cells, not to cells with metallic lithium deposits at high SOC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing anode-controlled onset temperature effects from cathode-controlled thermal runaway temperature in aged vs. fresh cells
analysis.primary_boundary: Anode degradation shifts onset but not TTR; TTR boundary 214–221°C holds across tested SOH and cycling conditions
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim on TTR depression by anode
regime transfer of SOC 0% TTR threshold to aged high-SOC cells
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing TTR reduction beyond tested SOH range
Claiming anode deposits dominate thermal runaway mechanism

solver_view.stem: Two cell populations are prepared: Population A cycled at 45°C (SOH 70%, >1100 cycles) and Population B cycled at 20°C (SOH 70%, <400 cycles). Both populations are tested at multiple SOC levels by accelerating rate calorimetry. Which statements about the onset temperature of exothermic reactions are correct?
options: A. Population A shows an average onset temperature near 99°C across all SOH levels tested, attributed to a thermally stable SEI impeding lithium de-intercalation.
B. Population B shows a higher average onset temperature than Population A because thicker SEI layers provide greater thermal insulation.
C. Population A's elevated onset temperature is caused by the thermally stable SEI delaying lithium de-intercalation, not by reduced lithium inventory.
D. Population B's onset temperature correlates strongly with SOC, similar to fresh cells, because the metallic lithium content is uniformly distributed.
E. The onset temperature difference between populations is primarily driven by cathode chemistry differences introduced by the two cycling temperatures.
F. Population B exhibits an earlier onset of exothermic reactions than fresh cells due to metallic lithium deposits identified by 7Li MAS NMR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states average onset of 99°C for 45°C-cycled cells across all SOHs, linked to stable SEI.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thick decomposition layer at 20°C is associated with lower, not higher, onset temperatures due to metallic lithium reactivity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Stable SEI mechanistically delays de-intercalation of lithium ions, directly causing the higher onset temperature in Population A.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Population B shows less pronounced SOC-onset correlation, opposite of this claim, due to metallic lithium acting independently of SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Onset temperature differences are attributed to anode SEI and lithium deposition differences, not cathode chemistry changes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 7Li MAS NMR confirmed metallic lithium in 20°C-cycled anodes, causally linked to earlier onset of exothermic reactions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism linking SEI quality and metallic lithium deposition to onset temperature of exothermic reactions
analysis.primary_boundary: Onset temperature governed by anode side; thick decomposition layer lowers onset, stable SEI raises it
intended_error_modes: direction reversal on thick SEI thermal effect
mechanism substitution attributing onset to cathode
direction reversal on SOC-onset correlation for aged cells
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cathode cycling-temperature effects drive onset differences
Generalizing Population A onset value beyond tested SOH range

solver_view.stem: During accelerating rate calorimetry of a fresh NMC-based lithium-ion cell at SOC 0%, self-heating rates remain below 1 K/min throughout the measurement range up to 300°C and no thermal runaway is observed. A colleague proposes that the same cell at SOC 100% will also avoid thermal runaway below 300°C because cathode decomposition onset near 200°C is the rate-limiting step regardless of SOC. Which options correctly evaluate this proposal?
options: A. The proposal is incorrect; SOC 0% cells show TTR > 300°C, but fully charged cells exhibit TTR in the 214–221°C range, well below 300°C.
B. The proposal is correct because cathode oxygen evolution above 200°C is the dominant thermal runaway trigger independent of SOC.
C. The proposal incorrectly treats the SOC 0% result as universally applicable; anode lithium content at SOC 100% enables earlier exothermic onset and lower TTR.
D. The proposal is partially valid because SEI breakdown near 100°C generates sufficient heat to trigger runaway before 200°C at any SOC.
E. The SOC 0% result cannot be extrapolated to SOC 100% because intercalated lithium in the anode contributes to exothermic reactions that lower the effective TTR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly places TTR for fully charged cells at 214–221°C, contrasting with TTR > 300°C for SOC 0% cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode decomposition is SOC-influenced; TTR differs substantially between SOC 0% and SOC 100%, refuting SOC-independence claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Anode intercalated lithium at high SOC participates in exothermic reactions, making SOC 0% extrapolation invalid for SOC 100%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI breakdown near 100°C produces low heat generation only; evidence does not support it triggering runaway at any SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Anode lithium content is a key variable; onset and TTR both depend on SOC, invalidating direct extrapolation from SOC 0%.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of extrapolating SOC 0% thermal runaway threshold to SOC 100% conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR > 300°C applies only to SOC 0%; fully charged cells have TTR 214–221°C
intended_error_modes: boundary overreach treating SOC 0% result as universal
causal overclaim that SEI breakdown alone triggers runaway
unsupported SOC-independence of cathode decomposition trigger
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying SOC 0% TTR threshold to intermediate or high SOC states
Claiming SEI breakdown is sufficient for thermal runaway initiation

solver_view.prompt: A lithium-ion cell is cycled at 20°C until severe capacity fading occurs, and post-mortem analysis reveals a thick decomposition layer containing metallic lithium on the anode surface. A second cell is cycled at 45°C to a comparable SOH and shows an effective, thermally stable SEI with no metallic lithium deposits. Explain, using anode-side mechanisms, why these two aging pathways produce different onset temperatures for exothermic reactions during heating, and identify which electrode controls the thermal runaway temperature and why the two cells converge at similar TTR values despite their different onset temperatures.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Cycling at 20°C produces a thick decomposition layer containing metallic ('mossy') lithium on the anode, confirmed by 7Li MAS NMR. Metallic lithium is chemically reactive at lower temperatures than intercalated lithium, causing earlier onset of exothermic reactions and weakening the SOC-onset correlation. Cycling at 45°C forms a thermally stable SEI that impedes lithium de-intercalation, raising the average onset temperature to approximately 99°C. Despite this difference in onset, thermal runaway temperature is controlled by the cathode: progressive cathode decomposition beginning near 200°C releases oxygen, initiating exothermic electrolyte oxidation that drives runaway. Because both cell populations share the same cathode chemistry and the moderate cycling conditions tested did not significantly alter cathode thermal stability, both converge to TTR in the narrow range of 214–221°C regardless of anode aging pathway.
must_include: metallic lithium deposits lower onset temperature in 20°C-cycled cells
stable SEI at 45°C delays lithium de-intercalation raising onset to ~99°C
thermal runaway is dominated by cathode decomposition and reaction with electrolyte
TTR falls in 214–221°C range for both cell types
cathode thermal stability was not significantly altered under the tested cycling conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming TTR would differ substantially if cycling conditions such as current density or cut-off potential were more aggressive without additional evidence
Generalizing the 99°C onset value to cell chemistries or cycling conditions outside those tested
Asserting that metallic lithium deposits alone are sufficient to trigger thermal runaway

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain linking anode aging mechanism to onset temperature, then identifying cathode as TTR determinant and explaining convergence
analysis.primary_boundary: Anode controls onset; cathode controls TTR; convergence explained by unchanged cathode stability under tested conditions
intended_error_modes: attributing TTR differences to anode degradation severity
claiming onset temperature predicts TTR magnitude
overgeneralizing cathode dominance to conditions with more aggressive cycling

solver_view.stem: An NCM lithium-ion battery is subjected to local high-temperature heating under varying surface heat dissipation coefficients (h). Tests show that at h = 10 W/(m²·K), thermal runaway is prevented. A thermal engineer proposes four statements about the effect of increasing h from 2 to 10 W/(m²·K). The heating area and temperature are held constant. Which statements are CORRECT?
options: A The pre-heat-runaway reactions (SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction) are suppressed and do not occur at h = 10 W/(m²·K).
B The heat of reaction from pre-heat-runaway reactions is greatly reduced as h increases, even though these reactions still occur.
C Temperature distribution uniformity across the separator layer improves as h increases during local heating.
D Cathode decomposition becomes the dominant heat-generating side reaction at higher h values.
E Thermal runaway prevention at h = 10 W/(m²·K) is attributable to improved separator temperature uniformity reducing internal short-circuit risk.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states increasing h does not prevent pre-heat-runaway reactions from occurring, only reduces their heat output.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states heat of reaction is greatly reduced with increasing h, though pre-heat-runaway reactions still occur.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator temperature distribution uniformity increases as surface heat dissipation coefficient increases during heating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states only SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction contribute heat; cathode decomposition shows no contribution under any tested condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While separator uniformity improves, the causal chain to thermal runaway prevention is not established this way; separator melting causing short circuit is the TR symbol, but the link to h preventing TR is stated without this intermediate mechanism being confirmed.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Effect of increasing heat dissipation coefficient on reaction occurrence, heat output, and separator uniformity
analysis.primary_boundary: h = 10 W/(m²·K) prevents TR but does not suppress pre-heat-runaway reaction occurrence
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring reaction suppression from TR prevention
Attributing dominant heat generation to cathode decomposition

solver_view.stem: Under a constant heating temperature applied to an NCM lithium-ion battery, the heated surface area is doubled. The heat dissipation coefficient remains unchanged. A researcher makes the following claims about the effect of this change. Which claims are CORRECT?
options: A The maximum temperature rise during thermal runaway increases proportionally with the larger heating area.
B The rate of active material consumption increases with the larger heating area.
C New side reactions, such as cathode decomposition or binder reaction, are activated by the larger heating area.
D The reaction rate of the active side reactions increases with the larger heating area.
E The types of side reactions contributing to heat generation remain unchanged when heating area is increased at constant heating temperature.
F Separator melting threshold temperature decreases when heating area is increased.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states maximum temperature rise does not change when heating area increases at constant heating temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states the rate of material consumption increases with increasing heating area at constant heating temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states type of side reactions does not change with heating area; only SEI and negative-electrolyte reactions contribute heat in all tested conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states reaction rate increases with increasing heating area under constant heating temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the type of side reaction does not change with increased heating area or temperature; only SEI and negative-electrolyte reactions are active.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links heating area to a change in separator melting threshold temperature; this is an unsupported extrapolation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing which thermal runaway parameters change vs. remain invariant when heating area increases at constant temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: Constant heating temperature scope: reaction type and max temperature rise unchanged; rate and consumption rate change
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring new reaction activation from larger area
Inferring max temperature rise scales with area

solver_view.prompt: An NCM battery thermal model includes five candidate side reactions. Across all tested conditions varying heating temperature, heat dissipation coefficient, and heating area, only two reactions contribute to heat generation. Separator melting is identified as the defining event of thermal runaway onset. A thermal management system achieves h = 10 W/(m²·K) at the battery surface. Explain: (1) which two reactions are active and why the others are excluded, (2) what physical event defines thermal runaway onset and its causal mechanism, and (3) what the h = 10 W/(m²·K) threshold achieves and what it does NOT prevent.

ground_truth.gold_answer: (1) SEI film decomposition and the negative-electrolyte reaction are the only two active side reactions across all tested conditions. Cathode decomposition, binder reaction, and electrolyte decomposition show no contribution to heat generation under any tested heating condition, indicating the thermal excursions reached in these tests are insufficient to activate those reactions or they do not contribute measurably within this experimental scope. (2) Separator melting defines thermal runaway onset because it causes internal short circuit, which produces a dramatic temperature increase — the melting is the significant symbol of TR. (3) At h = 10 W/(m²·K), thermal runaway is prevented, meaning separator melting and the associated temperature jump do not occur. However, this threshold does NOT prevent the pre-heat-runaway reactions (SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction) from occurring; it only greatly reduces their heat output.
must_include: SEI film decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction are the only active reactions
cathode decomposition, binder reaction, electrolyte decomposition show no heat contribution
separator melting causes internal short circuit leading to dramatic temperature rise
h = 10 W/(m²·K) prevents thermal runaway
h = 10 W/(m²·K) does not prevent pre-heat-runaway reactions from occurring
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming h = 10 W/(m²·K) suppresses SEI or negative-electrolyte reactions entirely
Claiming cathode decomposition is activated at higher heating areas or temperatures within this study
Generalizing the five-reaction model as universally complete for all NCM chemistries

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain from active reactions through separator melting to TR, combined with boundary reasoning about what h threshold achieves and does not achieve
analysis.primary_boundary: h = 10 W/(m²·K) prevents TR but not pre-TR reactions; only two of five reactions are active across all tested conditions
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution attributing TR onset to reaction heat rather than separator melting
scale transfer overclaim generalizing experimental reaction set universally

solver_view.stem: A 16Ah NCM523 soft-pack cell undergoes thermal runaway under three separate abuse conditions: external heating, overcharge, and nail penetration. An engineer compares the early-warning utility of voltage signals across these three conditions. Which of the following statements are supported by the experimental evidence?
options: A During heating TR, voltage remains stable for over 600 s before dropping to 0 V, making it a poor early-warning signal for this abuse mode.
B During overcharged TR, voltage drop precedes the catastrophic temperature change rate by 2 s, giving it a marginal but measurable early-warning advantage over temperature.
C During nail penetration TR, voltage provides a clear slow-change precursor phase before the rapid drop, enabling advance warning.
D During heating TR, voltage drop coincides precisely with the soft-package burst at 96.4 °C, confirming synchronized thermal-electrical failure.
E Voltage is a reliable early-warning signal across all three abuse conditions tested.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage stable ~607.2 s then abrupt drop; explicitly identified as failing to provide clear early warning.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage drop leads catastrophe-critical temperature rate by 2 s during overcharge; evidence-supported marginal advantage.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states no apparent slow change process for voltage during nail penetration TR; opposite of claimed.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not link voltage drop timing to the 96.4 °C burst event; unsupported causal synchronization claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Heating TR evidence explicitly shows voltage fails as early warning; universalizing across all modes is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Condition-dependent early-warning utility of voltage signal across three abuse modes
analysis.primary_boundary: Voltage warning validity is abuse-mode-specific; heating and nail penetration differ from overcharge
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing overcharge voltage-warning advantage to all abuse modes
Inferring voltage-temperature synchronization at burst without evidence

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway of 16Ah NCM523 under external heating, simulation identifies sequential decomposition events. The SEI film begins decomposing at 94.1 °C, the positive active material reacts with electrolyte near 324 °C, and electrolyte decomposition occurs near 360 °C. The soft-package burst is experimentally observed at 96.4 °C. Which conclusions are consistent with both the simulation thresholds and experimental observations?
options: A The soft-package burst at 96.4 °C coincides with the onset of SEI film decomposition, suggesting SEI decomposition contributes to early mechanical failure.
B Positive active material reaction with electrolyte is the first exothermic event to occur after the soft-package burst under heating conditions.
C Electrolyte decomposition initiates before the positive active material reacts with electrolyte during heating TR.
D The maximum temperature of 420.2 °C exceeds the simulation thresholds for both positive-electrolyte reaction and electrolyte decomposition, meaning both reactions likely occurred during the event.
E SEI decomposition onset temperature alone is sufficient to predict the maximum temperature reached during heating TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI onset 94.1 °C and burst 96.4 °C are within ~2 °C; temporal and thermal proximity supports the association.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Burst at 96.4 °C precedes 324 °C threshold; positive-electrolyte reaction is the next simulation-identified exotherm post-burst.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Simulation places positive-electrolyte reaction at ~324 °C and electrolyte decomposition at ~360 °C; order is reversed in this option.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Maximum temperature 420.2 °C exceeds both 324 °C and 360 °C thresholds, consistent with both reactions having occurred.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI onset temperature characterizes initiation only; maximum temperature depends on subsequent exotherms not captured by SEI threshold alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential reaction thresholds vs. experimental temperature milestones in heating TR
analysis.primary_boundary: Simulation thresholds apply to heating TR only; causal sufficiency of individual thresholds is not supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Predicting maximum temperature from SEI onset alone
Transferring heating TR reaction sequence to other abuse modes

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer compares nail penetration TR and overcharged TR for the same 16Ah NCM523 cell. Nail penetration produces a maximum temperature rise rate of 159 °C/s and peak temperature of 477.5 °C. Overcharge produces a maximum rate of 65 °C/s and peak temperature of 481.4 °C. The engineer concludes nail penetration is the more severe abuse mode. Which of the following correctly evaluates this conclusion and identifies the dominant heat source during overcharged TR?
options: A The conclusion is fully supported: nail penetration is more severe because both its temperature rise rate and peak temperature exceed those of overcharge.
B The conclusion is partially supported: nail penetration has a higher maximum temperature rise rate, but overcharge reaches a slightly higher peak temperature.
C The conclusion is unsupported: overcharge is more severe because peak temperature is the sole valid severity metric.
D During overcharged TR, heat primarily originates from positive electrode material reaction, which is identified as the main driver of temperature rise.
E During overcharged TR, electrolyte decomposition is the dominant heat source, consistent with its lower decomposition threshold relative to positive material reaction.
F Nail penetration TR is mechanistically initiated by separator multi-layer cracking due to shear forces, causing internal short circuit.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak temperature is higher for overcharge (481.4 vs 477.5 °C); rate is higher for nail penetration; conclusion is only partially correct.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Nail penetration rate (159 °C/s) exceeds overcharge (65 °C/s); overcharge peak (481.4 °C) exceeds nail penetration (477.5 °C); nuanced split is evidence-consistent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Designating peak temperature as sole severity metric is an unsupported methodological claim; evidence presents both metrics without ranking primacy.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly identifies Qpos as primary heat source and main cause of temperature rise during overcharged TR simulation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes dominant heat to positive electrode material reaction, not electrolyte decomposition, during overcharged TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states shear forces cause multi-layer separator cracks and faults leading to internal short circuit in nail penetration.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-metric severity comparison across abuse modes and dominant heat source identification
analysis.primary_boundary: Severity judgment requires both rate and peak temperature; no single metric is designated primary in evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Ranking overcharge vs nail penetration severity by single metric
Attributing dominant overcharge heat to electrolyte rather than positive material

solver_view.prompt: A 16Ah NCM523 cell undergoes overcharged thermal runaway. Engineers want to implement an early-warning system using onboard sensors. Based on the experimental and simulation evidence for this cell and abuse mode, identify the recommended warning threshold values, explain which sensor signal provides faster warning relative to temperature-based detection, and state why the same voltage-based warning strategy cannot be directly applied to heating-induced TR for this cell.

ground_truth.gold_answer: For overcharged TR, the critical warning thresholds are a surface temperature of 77 °C combined with a temperature rise rate of approximately 1 °C/s. Voltage provides a faster warning than temperature in overcharge: voltage begins dropping 2 s before the catastrophic temperature change rate is reached. This 2-second lead gives voltage marginal but measurable priority as an early-warning signal in overcharge. The same voltage strategy cannot be directly applied to heating-induced TR because, under heating, voltage remains stable for approximately 607.2 s before abruptly dropping to 0 V with no gradual precursor, providing no meaningful early warning. The abuse-mode-specific voltage behavior means warning logic must be conditioned on the triggering mechanism, not applied universally.
must_include: 77 °C and ~1 °C/s as overcharge warning thresholds
voltage leads temperature by 2 s in overcharge
voltage fails as early warning in heating TR due to ~607 s stable period before abrupt drop
warning strategy is abuse-mode-specific
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 77 °C threshold to heating or nail penetration TR
Claiming voltage is universally superior to temperature for TR warning
Inferring warning thresholds for nail penetration from overcharge data

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Abuse-mode-conditional early-warning strategy combining threshold values, signal priority, and boundary of applicability
analysis.primary_boundary: Warning thresholds and signal utility are specific to overcharged TR; heating TR voltage behavior is explicitly different
intended_error_modes: omission of rate threshold alongside temperature threshold

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion NCM/graphite pouch cell is aged by cycling at 50°C until it reaches 90% SOH, then subjected to a hard external short circuit (resistance ~0.7 mΩ). The surface temperature reaches 145.7°C when discharging current drops to zero. Which of the following statements correctly describe why this battery undergoes thermal runaway while batteries at 80% and 70% SOH do not, under the same ESC conditions?
options: A. At 90% SOH, heat from side reactions contributes more to total heat generation than Joule heat, and this side-reaction heat generation is higher than at 80% or 70% SOH.
B. At 90% SOH, Joule heat generation is the highest among all aged states because resistance is maximized at this degradation level.
C. The 90% SOH battery reaches a temperature at current cutoff that is sufficient to sustain exothermic side reactions autonomously, whereas lower-SOH batteries dissipate heat before this threshold.
D. Mn dissolution from the cathode during high-temperature cycling lowers the thermal runaway temperature, making the 90% SOH battery more susceptible than fresh cells.
E. The fast-discharging side-reaction heat generation effect peaks near 90% SOH and decreases with further SOH reduction, removing a key heat source in 80% and 70% SOH batteries.
F. Separator shrinkage at ~125°C causes micro internal short circuit in all aged batteries equally, so the 90% SOH outcome is solely determined by initial Joule heat differences.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states side reactions dominate total heat for 90% and 80% SOH batteries, and fast-discharging side-reaction heat peaks at 90% SOH.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Joule heat ranking is 100%>80%>90%≈70%; resistance increase affects Joule heat less, so 90% SOH does not have highest Joule heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 145.7°C when current reaches zero, residual high temperature drives further exothermic reactions leading to thermal runaway in the 90% SOH battery.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mn dissolution lowers thermal runaway temperature but this applies after high-temperature cycling generally, not uniquely explaining why 90% SOH is worse than 80% or 70%.

option_judgments.label: Correct
