Additionally, above 150°C, cathode decomposition can become self-sustaining if heat cannot be dissipated quickly enough; once this threshold is crossed, the reaction is no longer governed solely by external heat input, making cooling intervention less effective. Evidence explicitly states that extreme events such as cell rupture and short circuit may release energy beyond what a cooling system can mitigate. Therefore, cooling systems are necessary but insufficient as a sole safeguard; additional protections such as cell-level fusing, venting, and chemistry selection are required.
must_include: up to 70% of cell energy released in under one minute during short circuit
self-sustaining cathode decomposition above 150°C conditional on insufficient heat dissipation
cooling systems explicitly noted as insufficient sole safeguard for extreme events
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cooling systems provide zero benefit
Claiming thermal runaway is always self-sustaining above 150°C regardless of cooling
Extending conclusions to specific pack geometries or chemistries not mentioned in evidence

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-stage energy release rate vs. cooling capacity; conditionality of self-sustaining runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Cooling systems cannot mitigate energy release from rupture/short circuit events; self-sustaining runaway is conditional not universal

solver_view.stem: Two graphite-anode lithium-ion cells are aged to equivalent normalized usable capacity (UE = 80%): Cell A cycled at 25 °C / 2 C, Cell B cycled at 45 °C / 0.5 C. Both are then subjected to thermal abuse. Which statements about their thermal runaway (TR) behavior are supported by experimental evidence?
options: A. Cell A has a lower TR triggering temperature than Cell B, consistent with a negative correlation between C-rate and triggering temperature observed at 25 °C aging.
B. Cell B has a lower TR triggering temperature than Cell A because elevated-temperature aging always accelerates SEI degradation.
C. Cell B exhibits a higher TR triggering temperature than a cell aged at 45 °C / 0.2 C, reflecting non-monotonic C-rate dependence at 45 °C.
D. Both cells have identical post-triggering heat fractions (gTR) because UE is equalized.
E. Cell A poses heightened thermal hazard partly because low-temperature cycling promotes lithium plating, lowering the anode potential below 0 V vs. Li/Li+.
F. Lithium plating in Cell A fully accounts for the elevated heat generation observed after TR initiation near 250 °C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 25 °C, Ttr decreases from 254.6 °C at 0.2 C to 246.9 °C at 2 C, confirming negative correlation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Elevated-temperature aging stabilizes SEI and delays TR; the claim reverses the reported direction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 45 °C, Ttr rises from 256.0 °C at 0.2 C to 261.1 °C at 0.5 C before declining, so 0.5 C > 0.2 C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At UE = 80%, aged cells show gTR of 70–74% vs. 56.4% for fresh; equalizing UE does not equalize gTR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower cycling temperature lowers the lithium-plating threshold; plating accelerates early TR reactions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Plated-Li/electrolyte reaction peaks near 100 °C, far below ~250 °C TR trigger; additional energy sources are required.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-condition TR triggering temperature trends and mechanism boundaries for aged cells
analysis.primary_boundary: Non-monotonic C-rate dependence at 45 °C vs. monotonic at 25 °C; lithium-plating reaction temperature (~100 °C) vs. TR trigger (~250 °C)
disallowed_extrapolations: Equalizing UE equalizes thermal hazard metrics
Lithium plating alone explains post-TR heat generation

solver_view.stem: A battery engineer compares fresh and aged lithium-ion cells at equivalent normalized usable energy (UE = 80%) under thermal abuse. The aged cells were cycled at 20 °C. Which of the following conclusions are NOT supported by the available evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. Aged cells trigger TR at lower temperatures than fresh cells because aging universally degrades all protective interfaces.
B. The fraction of heat released after TR initiation (gTR) is higher in aged cells than in fresh cells at UE = 80%.
C. Elevated-temperature aging (45 °C) delays TR triggering relative to lower-temperature aging (20 °C) due to SEI stabilization.
D. Lithium plating at 20 °C cycling fully explains the elevated post-TR heat generation because plated lithium reacts exothermically near 250 °C.
E. Aged cells present higher overall thermal risk than fresh cells when compared at the same normalized usable capacity.
F. The exothermic plated-lithium/electrolyte reaction near 100 °C is the sole driver of TR initiation at ~250 °C in 20 °C-aged cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence links lower Ttr to specific aging conditions; 'universally degrades all protective interfaces' is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: gTR is 70–74% for aged vs. 56.4% for fresh at UE = 80%; this is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: 45 °C aging stabilizes SEI and delays TR; 20 °C aging promotes lithium plating and earlier TR—directly evidenced.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported)
option_judgments.justification: Plated-Li reaction occurs near 100 °C, not 250 °C; attributing full post-TR heat to this reaction at 250 °C is mechanistically wrong.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (supported)
option_judgments.justification: Aged cells show lower Ttr and higher heat generation at equivalent UE, confirming elevated thermal risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct (unsupported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states additional energy sources beyond plated-Li/electrolyte reaction must be considered; sole-driver claim is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment: distinguishing supported comparisons from unsupported universal or mechanistic overclaims
analysis.primary_boundary: Plated-Li reaction temperature (~100 °C) cannot explain TR trigger at ~250 °C; SEI stabilization is aging-temperature-specific
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal interface degradation claim
Lithium plating as sole post-TR heat source at 250 °C

solver_view.stem: Cells are aged under four conditions: (i) 25 °C / 0.2 C, (ii) 25 °C / 2 C, (iii) 45 °C / 0.2 C, (iv) 45 °C / 0.5 C. Thermal abuse tests measure TR triggering temperature (Ttr). Rank the following claims about Ttr ordering from most to least supported. Which claims are directly supported by measured data?
options: A. Ttr(iii) < Ttr(iv), because increasing C-rate at 45 °C always lowers Ttr.
B. Ttr(ii) < Ttr(i), because increasing C-rate at 25 °C correlates negatively with Ttr.
C. Ttr(iv) > Ttr(iii), consistent with a non-monotonic C-rate dependence at 45 °C where Ttr first rises before declining.
D. Ttr(i) ≈ Ttr(iii) because both use 0.2 C and C-rate is the dominant determinant of Ttr regardless of aging temperature.
E. Ttr(ii) < Ttr(iv) because 25 °C aging at 2 C promotes more lithium plating than 45 °C aging at 0.5 C, reducing Ttr further.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At 45 °C, Ttr rises from 0.2 C to 0.5 C before declining; monotonic decrease is contradicted by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Ttr drops from 254.6 °C at 0.2 C to 246.9 °C at 2 C at 25 °C; negative correlation is directly measured.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Ttr rises from 256.0 °C (0.2 C) to 261.1 °C (0.5 C) at 45 °C, directly supporting Ttr(iv) > Ttr(iii).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Aging temperature independently affects Ttr via SEI stabilization; C-rate alone does not determine Ttr.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 25 °C / 2 C promotes lithium plating (lower threshold, higher rate) lowering Ttr below 45 °C / 0.5 C where SEI is stabilized.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interaction of aging temperature and C-rate on TR triggering temperature, including non-monotonic behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: Non-monotonic Ttr vs. C-rate at 45 °C; aging temperature modifies the C-rate effect direction
disallowed_extrapolations: C-rate is the sole determinant of Ttr independent of aging temperature

solver_view.prompt: A researcher observes that cells aged at 20 °C exhibit earlier thermal runaway (TR) initiation than cells aged at 45 °C, and also that cells aged at 25 °C / 2 C show lower TR triggering temperatures than those aged at 25 °C / 0.2 C. The researcher proposes that lithium plating is the unifying mechanistic explanation for both observations, and further claims that the elevated post-TR heat generation in aged cells is fully explained by the exothermic reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte. Evaluate the mechanistic validity of this proposal, identifying where it is supported and where it exceeds the available evidence.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The proposal is partially supported but contains an unsupported overclaim. Lithium plating is a valid unifying mechanism for earlier TR initiation: lower cycling temperatures reduce the anode potential below 0 V vs. Li/Li+ more readily, promoting plating at 20 °C; higher C-rates at 25 °C similarly drive plating, and plated lithium reacts early with electrolyte to accelerate TR onset. This two-pathway explanation is evidence-consistent. However, the claim that plated-lithium/electrolyte reaction fully explains elevated post-TR heat generation is unsupported. That reaction predominantly occurs near 100 °C, far below the ~250 °C TR triggering temperature. Evidence explicitly states additional energy sources must be invoked to account for heat released after TR initiation. Therefore, lithium plating explains earlier initiation but cannot fully account for the magnitude of post-triggering heat generation.
must_include: Lithium plating threshold: anode potential below 0 V vs. Li/Li+
Lower temperature and higher C-rate both promote lithium plating
Plated-Li/electrolyte reaction occurs near 100 °C, not at ~250 °C TR trigger
Additional energy sources beyond lithium plating are required to explain post-TR heat
disallowed_extrapolations: Lithium plating alone fully explains post-TR heat generation
The 100 °C exothermic reaction directly drives TR initiation at 250 °C
Elevated-temperature aging always worsens TR risk

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary of lithium-plating mechanism: valid for TR initiation acceleration but insufficient to explain post-TR heat magnitude
analysis.primary_boundary: Plated-Li/electrolyte reaction temperature (~100 °C) vs. TR trigger temperature (~250 °C) creates a mechanistic gap requiring additional heat sources

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell with an NMC811 cathode and graphite anode is subjected to thermal abuse. The cell contains a PE-based separator and carbonate electrolyte. Which of the following statements correctly describe thermal events and their triggering conditions? Select all that apply.
options: A. SEI decomposition begins near 60°C, exposing lithiated graphite to electrolyte and initiating exothermic side reactions before separator failure occurs.
B. The PE-based separator melts near 130°C, which can cause internal short circuit.
C. NMC811 undergoes its first phase transition at approximately the same onset temperature as NMC111, around 260°C.
D. Electrolyte decomposition driven by carbonate solvent instability requires both temperature above 200°C and voltage near 4.6 V.
E. At 90°C, SEI completely decomposes and side reactions intensify, generating large amounts of gas and heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition onset at ~60°C exposes lithiated anode, initiating reactions before separator melts at 130°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PE-based separator melts above 130°C leading to internal short circuit, directly supported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: NMC811 first phase transition onset is ~135°C; NMC111 onset is ~260°C. The claim reverses or equates the two.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states electrolyte decomposes at >200°C and ~4.6 V due to carbonate solvents; both conditions are required.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 90°C SEI completely decomposes and side reactions intensify generating gases and heat, as stated in evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential thermal event thresholds and triggering conditions across cell components
analysis.primary_boundary: Temperature thresholds for SEI, separator, and electrolyte decomposition are component-specific and must not be conflated or swapped
intended_error_modes: regime transfer error between NMC811 and NMC111
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming electrolyte decomposition requires only temperature without voltage condition
Assuming NMC811 and NMC111 share the same onset temperature

solver_view.stem: A cell is deep-overdischarged until the anode potential reaches approximately 3.5 V vs. Li+/Li. The cell is then recharged. Separately, a cathode material LixCoO2 is charged to x = 0.14. Which statements correctly identify the failure mechanisms and their consequences? Select all that apply.
options: A. At anode potential ~3.5 V vs. Li+/Li, the copper current collector oxidizes, dissolving Cu2+ into electrolyte, which can deposit as dendrites on the cathode during subsequent charging.
B. Cu dendrite growth through the separator can cause internal short circuit and potentially trigger thermal runaway.
C. LixCoO2 with x = 0.14 is below the critical threshold of x = 0.16, so violent reaction with electrolyte releasing gas and heat is expected.
D. LixCoO2 with x = 0.14 is above the critical threshold and therefore reacts mildly with electrolyte under normal conditions.
E. Cu2+ ions deposit on the anode during overdischarge, directly causing short circuit without requiring a recharge step.
F. The copper oxidation mechanism requires the anode potential to exceed the copper oxidation potential, making this failure mode specific to deep overdischarge conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states Cu current collector oxidizes at ~3.5 V vs. Li+/Li, dissolving Cu2+ that migrates across separator.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states Cu dendrite growth penetrates separator causing self-short and potentially triggering thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: x = 0.14 < 0.16 critical threshold; evidence states below this value LixCoO2 reacts violently with electrolyte releasing gas and heat.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: x = 0.14 is below, not above, the critical threshold of 0.16; violent reaction is expected, not mild behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states Cu2+ deposits on cathode during subsequent charging, not on the anode during overdischarge itself.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Copper oxidation requires anode potential reaching ~3.5 V vs. Li+/Li, which occurs specifically under deep overdischarge.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chains for overdischarge-induced copper dissolution and LixCoO2 threshold-dependent reactivity
analysis.primary_boundary: Cu2+ deposition occurs on cathode during recharge, not on anode during discharge; x < 0.16 triggers violent reaction
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing Cu dendrite formation to all discharge conditions
Assuming LixCoO2 reaction severity is continuous rather than threshold-gated

solver_view.stem: During an overcharging experiment on a lithium-ion cell, a gas sensor and a temperature sensor are both monitoring the cell. The cell temperature remains below 60°C throughout the early overcharge phase. Additionally, lithium plating is occurring on the anode. Which statements are consistent with the evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. The gas sensor detects cell failure earlier than the temperature sensor while cell temperature is below 60°C.
B. The temperature sensor is the more reliable early-warning indicator when cell temperature is below 60°C during overcharge.
C. Lithium plating produces a volume increase of approximately 0.37 cm3 per Ah, which is larger than the ~0.12 cm3 volume change from intercalating 1 Ah into graphite.
D. Lithium plating and graphite intercalation produce equivalent volumetric changes per Ah, making them indistinguishable via mechanical monitoring.
E. Gas evolution during early overcharge below 60°C is consistent with electrolyte decomposition triggered above 200°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states gas sensor detected failure earlier than temperature sensor when cell temperature was below 60°C during overcharging.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows gas sensor outperforms temperature sensor below 60°C; temperature sensor is the inferior early-warning indicator here.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states lithium plating yields 0.37 cm3 Ah-1 vs. 0.12 cm3 per Ah for graphite intercalation; plating is larger.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows a threefold difference in volumetric change between plating and intercalation, making them mechanically distinguishable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte decomposition threshold is >200°C; gas evolution below 60°C cannot be attributed to that mechanism.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Early detection sensor hierarchy and volumetric discrimination between lithium plating and intercalation
analysis.primary_boundary: Gas sensor superiority is specific to below-60°C overcharge condition; electrolyte decomposition gas requires >200°C
intended_error_modes: regime transfer error attributing high-temperature gas mechanism to low-temperature observation
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming gas sensor is universally superior at all temperatures
Attributing early gas evolution to electrolyte decomposition without temperature condition being met

solver_view.prompt: A battery engineer is comparing NMC811 and NMC111 cathodes for a high-energy application. The engineer notes that NMC811 offers higher energy density but is concerned about thermal and mechanical stability. Using the evidence provided below as context: NMC811 has a phase transition onset near 135°C versus ~260°C for NMC111; NMC811 secondary particle volume change during cycling is 7.8% ± 1.5% versus 3.3% ± 2.4% for NMC111; and the presence of electrolyte reduces the onset decomposition temperature of delithiated NMC from 278°C to 197°C. Explain the two distinct stability disadvantages of NMC811 relative to NMC111, identify the mechanism responsible for the larger volumetric change in NMC811, and state what the electrolyte interaction data implies for safety-relevant testing conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: NMC811 has two stability disadvantages versus NMC111. First, its phase transition onset temperature (~135°C) is approximately 125°C lower than NMC111 (~260°C), meaning NMC811 enters thermally unstable regimes at much lower abuse temperatures, increasing thermal runaway risk. Second, NMC811 exhibits larger secondary particle volumetric change during cycling (7.8% ± 1.5% vs. 3.3% ± 2.4%), attributed to a pronounced decrease in the interlayer lattice parameter c (from 14.467 to 14.030 Å), which increases mechanical degradation risk over cycling. Regarding electrolyte interaction, the onset decomposition temperature of delithiated NMC drops from 278°C to 197°C in the presence of electrolyte, implying that safety-relevant thermal testing must include electrolyte to avoid overestimating actual decomposition onset temperatures.
must_include: NMC811 phase transition onset ~135°C vs NMC111 ~260°C
NMC811 volumetric change 7.8% vs NMC111 3.3%
lattice parameter c decrease as mechanism for volumetric change
electrolyte reduces onset decomposition temperature from 278°C to 197°C
testing without electrolyte overestimates thermal stability
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming NMC811 is always unsafe for all applications
Asserting volumetric change alone causes thermal runaway
Generalizing electrolyte effect to all NMC compositions without qualification
Claiming the 197°C threshold applies to non-delithiated NMC

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative thermal and mechanical stability of NMC811 vs NMC111 and implications of electrolyte interaction for safety testing
analysis.primary_boundary: Electrolyte effect on decomposition temperature is reported for delithiated Li1-x(Ni1/3Mn1/3Co1/3)O2 specifically; volumetric change mechanism is lattice parameter c contraction
intended_error_modes: scale transfer overclaim generalizing electrolyte effect to all NMC
causal overclaim linking volumetric change directly to thermal runaway
boundary overreach applying delithiated NMC data to fully lithiated state

solver_view.stem: A 62 Ah NMC/graphite prismatic cell undergoes thermal runaway in a confined two-battery system. Energy accounting shows total TR energy of 911,400 J. Conductive, convective, and radiative heat inputs to the secondary battery are 34,741 J, 3,260 J, and 5,162 J respectively. Which statements about heat transfer to the secondary battery are correct?
options: A Conductive heat transfer is the dominant pathway, contributing approximately 80% of total heat input to the secondary battery.
B Smoke-mediated convection and radiation together exceed conductive heat transfer as the primary propagation driver.
C Smoke-mediated pathways contribute less than 20% of direct heat input yet still accelerate propagation by suppressing heat dissipation from the secondary battery.
D Radiative heat from smoke exceeds convective heat from smoke in this confined configuration.
E Removing smoke from the confined space would eliminate thermal runaway propagation entirely.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 34,741 J / 43,163 J total = 80.5%, consistent with C002.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Smoke pathways total 19.5%, far below conduction at 80.5%; reverses reported hierarchy.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 explicitly states smoke elevates ambient temperature, inhibiting dissipation and accelerating propagation despite low direct heat share.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 5,162 J radiative > 3,260 J convective per C002, so radiation exceeds convection in this test.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Decoupled test still propagated via conduction (96.9% share per C006); smoke removal does not eliminate propagation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Relative contributions of heat transfer pathways and indirect smoke effect on propagation
analysis.primary_boundary: Confined two-battery configuration; 62 Ah NMC cell; quantitative shares from C002 and C004
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing pathway dominance to other cell chemistries or geometries
Claiming smoke removal prevents propagation

solver_view.stem: Two tests compare thermal runaway propagation in a two-battery system: a fully enclosed configuration and a battery-smoke decoupled configuration using the same 62 Ah NMC cells. Propagation times are 203.7 s (enclosed) and 369.9 s (decoupled). Ambient temperature after TR in the smoke-retaining test exceeded the smoke-vented test by 366.4°C. Which conclusions are supported?
options: A Smoke retention reduced propagation time by approximately 45% relative to the decoupled case.
B The 366.4°C ambient temperature elevation primarily increased direct radiative and convective heat input, which alone explains the faster propagation.
C Elevated ambient temperature from smoke retention reduces the thermal gradient driving heat loss from the secondary battery, contributing to faster propagation beyond direct heat addition.
D In the decoupled configuration, conduction still dominated heat transfer to the secondary battery at roughly 97%.
E Smoke retention effects are negligible because convection and radiation together supply less than 20% of trigger heat.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: (369.9-203.7)/369.9 = 44.9% reduction, matching C001 directly.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C004 states the key mechanism is inhibited heat dissipation, not solely increased direct input; overclaims direct-input causation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 explicitly links elevated Te to suppressed heat dissipation from secondary battery, accelerating propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C006 reports 96.9% conductive share in decoupled test, confirming conduction dominance without smoke.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Low direct heat share does not make smoke negligible; indirect dissipation suppression effect is evidenced in C004.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism by which smoke retention accelerates propagation beyond direct heat input
analysis.primary_boundary: Two-battery confined vs. decoupled configuration; same cell chemistry and format
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming smoke alone causes propagation
Generalizing time reduction percentage to other configurations

solver_view.stem: A 62 Ah NMC/graphite prismatic cell is characterized by ARC: self-heating onset 96.5°C, TR trigger temperature 189.6°C, maximum temperature 1086.9°C, max dT/dt > 800°C/s. Total TR energy is 911,400 J; 48% exits with ejected gases and particulates. Which statements are supported by this characterization?
options: A The cell enters self-heating roughly 93°C below the thermal runaway trigger temperature.
B More than half of total TR energy is retained as residual heat within the cell after venting.
C The maximum temperature rise rate indicates TR is effectively instantaneous, making propagation prevention impossible once onset is detected at 96.5°C.
D Ejected gases and particulates carry away the majority of TR energy, leaving less than half as residual cell heat.
E The 96.5°C onset temperature represents the threshold above which immediate thermal runaway is inevitable.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 189.6 - 96.5 = 93.1°C gap between onset and TR trigger, directly calculable from C007.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 52.0% residual heat per C005 exceeds 50%, so majority is retained in cell.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: High dT/dt describes TR phase only; onset at 96.5°C is self-heating start, not immediate TR; prevention window exists between 96.5°C and 189.6°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 48% exits via ejecta, leaving 52% residual; ejecta share is less than half, reversing the actual finding.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: 96.5°C is self-heating onset, not TR trigger; TR trigger is 189.6°C; onset does not imply inevitable immediate TR.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct interpretation of ARC threshold temperatures and energy partition
analysis.primary_boundary: 62 Ah NMC/graphite prismatic cell; ARC test conditions; defined temperature labels T1, T2, T3
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing ARC thresholds to other SOC levels or cell formats
Claiming onset temperature guarantees TR without reaching trigger temperature

solver_view.prompt: In a confined two-battery system using 62 Ah NMC cells, smoke-mediated heat transfer (convection + radiation) supplies only 19.5% of the direct heat input triggering thermal runaway in the secondary battery, yet the fully enclosed system propagates 44.9% faster than the smoke-decoupled system. Explain the mechanism that reconciles these two observations. Your answer should identify the physical pathway through which smoke accelerates propagation despite its small direct heat contribution.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Although smoke convection and radiation contribute only 19.5% of the heat directly absorbed by the secondary battery, smoke retained in the confined space raises the ambient temperature by 366°C relative to the vented case. This elevated ambient temperature reduces the thermal gradient between the secondary battery and its surroundings, thereby suppressing heat dissipation from the secondary battery. Reduced heat loss causes heat to accumulate within the secondary battery faster, advancing it toward the TR trigger temperature (189.6°C) more quickly. The acceleration mechanism is therefore indirect: smoke acts primarily as a thermal blanket that impairs cooling rather than as a dominant direct heat source, which explains why a small direct heat share can still produce a large reduction in propagation time.
must_include: elevated ambient temperature from smoke retention
inhibited or suppressed heat dissipation from secondary battery
heat accumulation in secondary battery
indirect mechanism distinct from direct heat input
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming smoke convection/radiation alone drives the 44.9% reduction
Claiming propagation would be prevented if smoke were removed
Generalizing this mechanism to open or large-format module configurations without qualification

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Indirect thermal mechanism by which smoke retention accelerates propagation despite low direct heat share
analysis.primary_boundary: Confined two-battery system; 62 Ah NMC cells; quantitative shares from C002 and C004; ambient temperature elevation from C003
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim attributing acceleration solely to direct radiative/convective input
boundary_overreach generalizing to all confined battery systems

solver_view.stem: An NCM523/graphite pouch cell uses a dual-salt electrolyte and undergoes one formation cycle, ending at 0% SOC. ARC testing shows thermal runaway with Tonset=141°C. A second cell uses the same dual-salt electrolyte but skips the formation process entirely. Which statements correctly describe the thermal behavior difference and its mechanistic basis?
options: A The unformed cell does not exhibit severe exothermic reactions below 250°C because LiH has not yet been generated in the SEI layer.
B The formed cell's early thermal runaway is primarily triggered by O2 release from the delithiated NCM523 cathode reacting with electrolyte above 141°C.
C LiH in the SEI layer reacts exothermically with the dual-salt electrolyte starting near 70°C, linking formation cycling to early thermal runaway onset.
D The unformed cell is thermally safer because it lacks both the dual-salt electrolyte and the formation-induced SEI species.
E Phase transformation of lithiated graphite is the root cause of thermal runaway in the formed cell, not an accelerating factor.
F H2 generated at the anode side can migrate to the cathode side and lower the cathode/electrolyte thermal onset temperature, contributing to runaway escalation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Without formation cycling, LiH is not generated; its absence explains why the unformed cell shows no severe exotherm below 250°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: O2 release from NCM523 is an accelerating factor, not the trigger; the root cause is LiH-induced H2 release.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LiH/electrolyte exothermic reaction starts at ~70°C and is identified as the critical contributor to early thermal runaway triggering.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The unformed cell retains the dual-salt electrolyte; the safety difference is specifically attributed to absence of formation-induced LiH, not absence of electrolyte.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly classifies graphite phase transformation as an accelerating factor, not the root cause.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: H2 migration from anode to cathode side lowers cathode/electrolyte Tonset from 136 to 116°C, escalating thermal runaway severity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing root-cause trigger from accelerating factors and linking formation-induced SEI species to early thermal runaway onset
analysis.primary_boundary: Formed vs. unformed cell with dual-salt electrolyte; LiH as trigger vs. O2/phase-change as accelerators
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing O2-release as primary trigger across all NCM chemistries
Claiming formation cycling alone without dual-salt electrolyte produces equivalent LiH

solver_view.stem: In an NCM523/graphite cell with dual-salt electrolyte, D2O titration detects HD signal at both 100% SOC and 0% SOC graphite anodes. Separately, two-bomb calorimetry shows that introducing anode-side gas species into a cathode/electrolyte system drops Tonset from 136°C to 116°C and produces a temperature spike from 159°C to 285°C. Which conclusions are supported by these findings?
options: A LiH in the SEI is electrochemically reversible, persisting at both fully lithiated and fully delithiated states.
B LiH is consumed irreversibly during delithiation, so HD signal at 0% SOC must originate from a different SEI species.
C H2 produced at the anode worsens cathode thermal stability even when the cathode has not yet begun its own decomposition.
D The 20°C drop in cathode Tonset is caused solely by direct electrolyte oxidation at the cathode surface, independent of anode gases.
E Anode-side gas migration represents a cross-electrode coupling mechanism that amplifies thermal runaway severity beyond what anode or cathode alone would produce.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: HD signal detected at both 0% and 100% SOC confirms LiH persists through cycling, demonstrating high electrochemical reversibility.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows HD signal at 0% SOC is consistent with LiH reversibility, not a different species; this reverses the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Two-bomb testing shows anode gas introduction lowers cathode Tonset to 116°C, indicating destabilization before cathode self-decomposition onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The Tonset drop is attributed to anode-side gas species (especially H2) migrating to cathode side, not independent cathode surface oxidation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: H2 migration creating a 20°C Tonset drop and amplified temperature spike demonstrates cross-electrode coupling that escalates overall runaway severity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Cross-electrode H2 migration as a coupling mechanism and LiH reversibility as evidence of persistent SEI species
analysis.primary_boundary: Anode-side gas effects on cathode stability measured via two-bomb calorimetry; SOC-independent LiH detection
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming H2 migration mechanism applies universally to all electrolyte chemistries without dual-salt context
Inferring cathode decomposition is fully suppressed by H2 alone

solver_view.prompt: In an NCM523/graphite pouch cell with dual-salt electrolyte, explain the sequential causal chain from LiH formation in the SEI to full thermal runaway, distinguishing root-cause triggers from accelerating factors. Include the relevant temperature thresholds and the role of cross-electrode gas migration in your answer.

ground_truth.gold_answer: LiH forms in the graphite anode SEI during formation cycling and persists reversibly across SOC states. Upon heating, LiH reacts exothermically with the dual-salt electrolyte starting at approximately 70°C, releasing H2—this is the root-cause trigger. H2 migrates from the anode to the cathode side, lowering cathode/electrolyte Tonset from 136°C to 116°C and producing a sharp temperature rise from 159°C to 285°C. This cross-electrode coupling escalates severity. Phase transformation of lithiated graphite and O2 release from delithiated NCM523 are accelerating factors, not triggers. The full runaway in a formed cell shows Tonset=141°C, Ttr=199°C, Tmax=280°C, absent in unformed cells lacking SEI-resident LiH.
must_include: LiH reacts with dual-salt electrolyte starting at ~70°C
H2 migration lowers cathode/electrolyte Tonset from 136 to 116°C
phase transformation and O2 release are accelerating factors, not root-cause triggers
formation cycling is required for LiH-driven runaway to occur
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming O2 release is the primary trigger
Asserting mechanism applies to cells without dual-salt electrolyte or without formation cycling
Generalizing LiH/electrolyte reaction onset temperature to other electrolyte systems

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Full causal chain reconstruction from SEI-resident LiH through cross-electrode H2 migration to thermal runaway, with correct trigger vs. accelerator classification
analysis.primary_boundary: NCM523/graphite with dual-salt electrolyte after formation cycling; temperature thresholds specific to this system
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim attributing runaway to O2 release
boundary overreach applying findings to unformed or different-electrolyte cells

solver_view.stem: A prismatic LFP battery at 100% SOC is subjected to bottom heating with a fixed heating quantity (BH–F) at 200 W for 1575 s. The maximum average battery temperature reached is 174.5°C. After the external heater is interrupted, a slight drop in the heat generation curve is observed. Which of the following statements are correct regarding TR triggering and post-heater-off behavior in this configuration?
options: A The battery does not spontaneously trigger TR because the heating-zone temperature never exceeds 200°C under 200 W fixed heating.
B TR is triggered by the decrease of the temperature gradient within the battery rather than by continued external heating.
C The post-heater-off heat generation drop indicates exothermic cathode decomposition accelerating after venting.
D Safety venting and TR occur simultaneously under BH–F because external heating is continuous.
E The endothermic process observed after safety venting is attributed to separator melting across multiple layers.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C007 states 174.5°C is below the 200°C threshold required to initiate intense exothermic reactions and trigger TR spontaneously.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C004 explicitly states TR under BH–F is triggered by decreasing temperature gradient, not direct external heating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C008 identifies the post-heater-off drop as endothermic separator melting, not exothermic cathode decomposition. Mechanism substitution error.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C003 states simultaneous SV and TR occur under BH–C (continuous), not BH–F; BH–F has insufficient external heating. Regime transfer error.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C008 directly attributes the post-venting endothermic drop to separator melting across multiple layers.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: TR triggering mechanism under fixed heating quantity and post-venting thermal behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: BH–F regime at 200 W; 200°C threshold for exothermic initiation
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending BH–C simultaneous SV/TR behavior to BH–F
Assuming post-venting drop is exothermic

solver_view.stem: An engineer compares bottom heating continuous (BH–C) and front heating continuous (FH–C) modes for prismatic LFP batteries at 100% SOC under 500 W. BH–C yields a peak temperature of 428.7°C and max temperature rise rate of 5.1°C/s; FH–C yields 359.1°C and 3.7°C/s. Internal heat generation before TR accounts for more than 35% of temperature increase under bottom heating. Which statements are supported by this evidence?
options: A Bottom heating produces more severe TR than front heating across peak temperature, temperature rise rate, and heat generation metrics.
B The higher severity under BH–C is solely due to greater external power input, since both modes use the same heater power.
C Internal self-heating before TR onset is a non-negligible contributor to reaching TR conditions under bottom heating.
D Front heating is safer because heat is applied to a surface with lower electrochemical activity, a mechanism confirmed for all LFP geometries.
E The difference in peak temperatures between BH–C and FH–C exceeds 60°C, consistent with bottom heating being the more severe mode.
F Internal heat generation exceeding 35% contribution implies external heating alone is insufficient to trigger TR under bottom heating fixed quantity at low power.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C001 and C010 confirm BH–C exceeds FH–C on all cited severity metrics at 500 W, 100% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C002 shows internal heat generation contributes >35%, so severity difference is not solely from external power. Causal overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C002 explicitly states internal heat generation before TR is non-negligible, exceeding 35% contribution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not provide a mechanistic explanation based on electrochemical activity location, nor does it generalize to all LFP geometries. Boundary overreach.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 428.7 − 359.1 = 69.6°C > 60°C, directly verifiable from C010 data, consistent with C001 severity ranking.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C007 shows 200 W and 100 W BH–F fail to reach 200°C threshold; combined with C002, internal heating contribution is needed but insufficient at low power.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Relative TR severity between heating modes and role of internal heat generation
analysis.primary_boundary: 500 W continuous heating, 100% SOC, prismatic LFP; BH–F low-power regime distinct from BH–C
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing heating-face electrochemical activity mechanism beyond evidence
Attributing severity difference entirely to external power

solver_view.stem: For prismatic LFP batteries under bottom heating fixed quantity (BH–F), TR onset time follows y = ax^b (power-law) with heating power. At 200 W and 100 W (1575 s heating), average temperatures of 174.5°C and 162.2°C are reached without spontaneous TR. The 200°C threshold must be exceeded in the heating zone to initiate TR. Which statements correctly identify why TR does not occur at low heating power and what the power-law implies?
options: A At low heating power, longer heating duration increases heat dissipation, requiring greater total external energy to reach the 200°C threshold.
B The power-law implies TR onset time decreases linearly as heating power decreases below 200 W.
C TR onset time increasing exponentially at low power is consistent with the power-law relationship y = ax^b where b is negative.
D Because average temperature at 100 W is 162.2°C, the local heating-zone temperature also cannot exceed 200°C, confirming no TR.
E Insufficient heating power prevents the heating-zone temperature from exceeding 200°C, which is necessary to initiate intense exothermic reactions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C007 explicitly states long heating time at low power causes more heat dissipation, requiring more external energy to trigger TR.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: C006 states onset time increases exponentially (not linearly) as power decreases. Reverses the functional form.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A power-law y = ax^b with b negative produces exponentially increasing onset time as x decreases, consistent with C006.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Average temperature below 200°C does not confirm local heating-zone temperature is also below 200°C; scale transfer overclaim from average to local.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C005 requires heating-zone temperature >200°C for intense exothermic reactions; C007 confirms this is not reached at 100–200 W.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Power-law TR onset scaling and low-power TR suppression mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: BH–F regime; 200°C heating-zone threshold; power range 100–500 W
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring local heating-zone temperature from average temperature
Treating power-law as linear relationship

solver_view.prompt: A post-mortem calorimetric measurement of a prismatic LFP battery that has undergone thermal runaway yields a specific heat capacity of 8.89 J/g·K, compared to approximately 1.1 J/g·K for a normal battery. Separately, under bottom heating fixed quantity (BH–F), an endothermic process is observed after safety venting, attributed to separator melting. Using these two observations, explain: (1) what the large increase in specific heat capacity of the failed battery implies about its physical state, and (2) why the endothermic separator melting observation is mechanistically significant for understanding TR progression under BH–F conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: (1) The ~8× increase in specific heat capacity of the failed battery (8.89 vs ~1.1 J/g·K) indicates that thermal runaway has substantially altered the battery's internal material composition and structure. A higher specific heat capacity means the post-TR residue absorbs more energy per degree of temperature rise, consistent with decomposition products and structural changes replacing the original electrode and separator materials. (2) Separator melting after safety venting under BH–F is endothermic, meaning it temporarily absorbs heat rather than releasing it. This is mechanistically significant because it represents a transient energy sink that could delay or modulate the temperature rise needed to sustain TR progression. Under BH–F, where external heating is already limited and the 200°C threshold must be exceeded locally to initiate intense exothermic reactions, this endothermic phase competes with heat accumulation, making TR triggering dependent on whether subsequent exothermic reactions overcome this energy absorption.
must_include: specific heat capacity increase implies altered post-TR material state
8.89 vs ~1.1 J/g·K comparison
endothermic separator melting acts as a heat sink
mechanistic relevance to TR progression under limited external heating (BH–F)
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming the specific heat capacity change predicts TR severity in normal batteries
Asserting separator melting prevents TR universally
Generalizing BH–F separator melting behavior to BH–C or front heating modes without qualification

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Physical interpretation of post-TR calorimetric change and mechanistic role of endothermic separator melting in TR progression
analysis.primary_boundary: Post-TR failed battery state; BH–F regime with limited external heating and 200°C local threshold requirement
intended_error_modes: boundary overreach from failed-battery properties to normal-battery predictions
causal overclaim that endothermic melting prevents TR
regime transfer from BH–F to BH–C

solver_view.stem: A graphite-anode lithium-ion cell undergoes sudden death under four degradation paths: low-temperature cycling (path-L), high-rate cycling (path-F), high-temperature cycling (path-H), and high-temperature calendar aging (path-R). Which of the following statements about the proximate cause of lithium plating are correct for the stated paths?
options: A Under path-L and path-F, limited lithium intercalation rate into graphite is the primary cause of lithium plating on the anode surface.
B Under path-H and path-R, limited lithium intercalation rate is also the primary cause of lithium plating, identical to path-L and path-F.
C Under path-H and path-R, gas generation between electrodes causes localized anode drying and uneven potential distribution, leading to lithium plating.
D Under path-L, worsened kinetic conditions delay lithium-ion intercalation into graphite, while under path-F, insufficient intercalation speed at high rate produces the same plating outcome.
E Under path-H and path-R, lithium plating is caused solely by electrolyte decomposition at the cathode, not by anode-side gas effects.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states limited intercalation rate is the primary cause under both path-L and path-F.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Path-H and path-R have a distinct mechanism (gas-induced drying and uneven potential), not limited intercalation rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly attributes path-H and path-R lithium plating to gas-induced localized drying and uneven anode potential.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence distinguishes kinetic worsening (path-L) from rate-limited intercalation speed (path-F) while identifying the same root cause.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes path-H and path-R plating to anode-side gas effects, not cathode electrolyte decomposition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing proximate causes of lithium plating across four degradation paths
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism differs between kinetic-limited paths (L, F) and gas-driven paths (H, R)
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming a single universal plating mechanism across all paths

solver_view.stem: After sudden death, the self-heating initial temperature T1 drops from 115.7°C to 53.3°C, 53.9°C, 63.9°C, and 59.4°C under path-L, path-F, path-H, and path-R respectively. Incremental capacity (IC) curve peaks shift toward higher voltage under path-L and path-F but toward lower voltage under path-H and path-R. Which statements correctly link these observations to underlying mechanisms?
options: A The larger T1 drop under path-L and path-F is consistent with more severe SEI thermal stability reduction compared to path-H and path-R.
B The IC peak shift toward higher voltage under path-L and path-F indicates that sudden death primarily disrupts the charge transfer process in those paths.
C The IC peak shift toward lower voltage under path-H and path-R reflects that sudden death primarily affects the charge transfer process, altered by solid-liquid-gas three-phase conditions.

D The smaller T1 drop under path-H and path-R is partly explained by plated lithium reacting rapidly with electrolyte at high temperature, forming metastable products that marginally preserve anode thermal stability.
E The T1 drop magnitude is determined solely by capacity fade, making path-L and path-H equivalent in thermal risk after sudden death.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI thermal stability reduction is more severe under path-L and path-F, consistent with larger T1 drops.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence assigns higher-voltage IC peak shift to anode interface polarization, not charge transfer disruption, under path-L and path-F.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence links lower-voltage IC shift under path-H and path-R to charge transfer disruption via solid-liquid-gas three-phase transition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states rapid high-temperature reaction of plated lithium forms metastable products, marginally preserving thermal stability under path-H and path-R.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows path-L and path-H have different T1 values (53.3°C vs 63.9°C) and different mechanisms; capacity fade alone is not cited as the sole determinant.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Linking T1 drop magnitude and IC peak direction to distinct degradation mechanisms across paths
analysis.primary_boundary: IC peak direction differs by path group; SEI stability and T1 trends are correlated but mechanistically distinct
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating T1 as determined solely by capacity fade
Generalizing charge-transfer disruption to path-L and path-F

solver_view.prompt: A cell that has undergone sudden death under high-temperature cycling (path-H) shows a smaller reduction in self-heating initial temperature T1 compared to a cell degraded under low-temperature cycling (path-L), even though lithium plating is identified as the primary failure mechanism in both cases. Explain the mechanistic reasons for this difference in thermal stability outcome, referencing both the cause of lithium plating and the subsequent behavior of plated lithium under each condition.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Under path-L, worsened kinetic conditions delay lithium-ion intercalation into graphite, causing lithium to plate on the anode surface. This plated lithium persists in a relatively reactive state, severely degrading SEI thermal stability and producing a large T1 drop. Under path-H, lithium plating arises from a different mechanism: gas generation obstructs intercalation sites, causes localized anode drying, and creates uneven potential distribution. Critically, at high temperature the plated lithium rapidly reacts with electrolyte to form metastable side-reaction products. These products marginally preserve anode thermal stability compared to path-L, resulting in a smaller T1 drop. The difference is therefore rooted in both the distinct plating trigger and the temperature-dependent reactivity of plated lithium post-deposition.
must_include: limited intercalation rate as path-L plating cause
gas generation and localized drying as path-H plating cause
rapid reaction of plated lithium with electrolyte at high temperature forming metastable products
smaller T1 drop under path-H linked to marginally higher anode thermal stability
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming path-H cells are thermally safe or that T1 reduction is negligible
Asserting that metastable products fully restore SEI stability
Generalizing the metastable-product mechanism to path-L or path-F

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-hop causal chain linking plating mechanism to post-plating chemistry to thermal stability outcome across two paths
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanistic difference between kinetic-limited (path-L) and gas-driven (path-H) plating, and temperature-dependent fate of plated lithium
intended_error_modes: boundary overreach claiming path-H is thermally safe
ignoring the role of plated-lithium reactivity in thermal outcome

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery undergoes nail penetration at 1 mm/s. During the first 1–2 s, voltage drops to ~0.5 V and temperature reaches ~110 °C. In the subsequent 2–3 s period, voltage and temperature increase rather than continuing to fall. Which of the following statements correctly explain this behavior? Select all that apply.
options: A. The voltage and temperature increase in the 2–3 s period is attributed to triggering of the self-polymerized SEI on the cathode at 110–113 °C.
B. The voltage recovery indicates the internal short circuit has self-healed due to nail withdrawal.
C. The temperature rise in the 2–3 s period occurs while the nail is still penetrating, not after it has been removed.
D. The initial voltage drop to ~0.5 V reflects the onset of internal short-circuit current before the cathode SEI reaction activates.
E. The SEI polymerization reaction on the cathode is the primary driver of the voltage and temperature increase in the 2–3 s window.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states cathode SEI triggered at 110–113 °C in 2–3 s period caused voltage and temperature increase.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports self-healing of short circuit; nail is still penetrating during this period, not withdrawn.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states voltage and temperature increase 'as the nail continued its penetration,' confirming nail is still moving.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage drop to ~0.5 V in 1–2 s precedes SEI activation, consistent with short-circuit current before cathode reaction triggers.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence labels cathode SEI as 'primary' trigger for voltage and temperature increase in the 2–3 s period.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential mechanism of voltage and temperature evolution during nail penetration, distinguishing short-circuit onset from SEI-triggered secondary rise.
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are specific to 1 mm/s penetration speed and the defined 1–2 s and 2–3 s time windows.
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing timing to other penetration speeds
Assuming nail withdrawal causes voltage recovery

solver_view.stem: A battery electrolyte contains 1.5 wt% branched oligomer additive. Positron annihilation measurements show free volume decreases from 145.3 Å³ to 130.4 Å³ (a 0.22% reduction in free volume fraction). Discharge capacity at 1 C in a full cell is reduced by ~3.6%; charge capacity and OCP are unaffected. Which conclusions are supported by this evidence? Select all that apply.
options: A. The 15 Å³ free volume reduction confirms that the additive participates in a self-polymerized electrochemical reaction forming a dense SEI.
B. The 0.22% free volume reduction is large enough to fully block ionic transport, explaining the 3.6% capacity loss.
C. The discharge capacity reduction at 1 C is attributed to resistance encountered by lithium ions intercalating into the cathode through the thick SEI.
D. Because charge capacity and OCP are unaffected, the additive-formed SEI does not impede lithium de-intercalation from the anode.
E. The free volume decrease demonstrates that the additive forms SEI on the cathode rather than the anode.
F. The 3.6% discharge capacity reduction indicates the additive is unsuitable for any practical battery application.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states the 0.22% free volume decrease 'confirms a self-polymerized electrochemical reaction in the formation of SEI.'

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes ionic transformation as not fully blocked; 'slight' SEI may not affect it. Full blockage is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states lithium ions intercalating to cathode 'encountered resistance in the thick cathode SEI,' directly explaining capacity reduction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states charge capacity and OCP are unaffected, and de-intercalation from anode proceeds normally; SEI resistance is cathode-side.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Free volume measurement alone does not localize SEI to cathode vs. anode; cathode location is inferred from capacity/current evidence, not free volume alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes 3.6% as a 'slight effect'; no conclusion about practical unsuitability is drawn. This is an unsupported universal claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Linking free volume reduction to SEI formation mechanism and connecting cathode SEI resistance to asymmetric capacity effects.
analysis.primary_boundary: Free volume data is at 1.5 wt% additive only; capacity effect is at 1 C in full cell configuration.
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring full ionic blockage from 0.22% free volume change
Localizing SEI solely from free volume data

solver_view.stem: After complete nail penetration (27–100 s stage), a battery with branched oligomer additive protection shows voltage drop to 0 V and temperature stabilization at ~100 °C with no smoke or explosion. A battery without additive is not described in this stage. Which of the following statements are admissible based on this evidence alone?
options: A. The additive-protected battery avoids thermal runaway during the post-penetration stage under the tested conditions.
B. The additive suppresses thermal runaway in all nail penetration scenarios regardless of nail speed or SOC.
C. Voltage dropping to 0 V after complete penetration is consistent with full internal short-circuit discharge, not thermal runaway propagation.
D. The absence of smoke or explosion at ~100 °C in the additive battery demonstrates that the cathode SEI formed during 2–3 s provides continued protection post-penetration.
E. Without additive, the battery would necessarily produce smoke or explosion under identical test conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states additive-protected battery remained at ~100 °C without smoke or explosion after complete penetration under tested conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence covers only 1 mm/s penetration at one condition; generalizing to all speeds and SOC is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Voltage at 0 V after complete penetration indicates full discharge through short circuit; no evidence links this to runaway propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not establish a causal link between the 2–3 s SEI formation event and the post-penetration thermal stability outcome.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence describes the no-additive battery behavior in the 27–100 s stage; this is an unsupported counterfactual inference.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility judgment on post-penetration safety claims, distinguishing evidence-bounded conclusions from unsupported generalizations and counterfactuals.
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations limited to additive-protected battery at specified test conditions; no-additive post-penetration data not provided.
disallowed_extrapolations: Comparing additive vs. no-additive outcome in 27–100 s stage
Attributing post-penetration stability causally to 2–3 s SEI event without evidence

solver_view.prompt: During nail penetration testing at 1 mm/s, a battery with 1.5 wt% branched oligomer additive shows the following sequence: (1) voltage drops to ~0.5 V and temperature reaches ~110 °C in 1–2 s; (2) voltage and temperature then increase during 2–3 s; (3) after complete penetration, voltage falls to 0 V and temperature stabilizes at ~100 °C without smoke or explosion. Free volume measurements show the additive reduces free volume by 15 Å³ (0.22% fraction). Discharge capacity at 1 C is reduced by ~3.6% while charge capacity and OCP are unaffected. Explain the mechanistic role of the additive across these three stages, identifying what each piece of evidence supports and what remains outside the scope of the evidence.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Stage 1 (1–2 s): The initial voltage drop to ~0.5 V and temperature rise to ~110 °C reflect internal short-circuit onset from nail contact; the additive is not yet implicated as the primary actor here. Stage 2 (2–3 s): At 110–113 °C, the additive triggers self-polymerization on the cathode SEI, causing voltage and temperature to increase as penetration continues. The 15 Å³ free volume reduction and 0.22% fraction decrease confirm dense SEI formation via this electrochemical reaction. The 3.6% discharge capacity loss at 1 C is consistent with lithium-ion resistance at the thick cathode SEI during intercalation; charge capacity and OCP are unaffected, indicating anode-side de-intercalation is not impeded. Stage 3 (27–100 s): The additive-protected battery stabilizes at ~100 °C without smoke or explosion. However, the evidence does not establish a direct causal link between the Stage 2 SEI event and Stage 3 stability, nor does it describe the no-additive battery outcome in this stage for comparison.
must_include: self-polymerization of cathode SEI triggered at 110–113 °C in 2–3 s period
15 Å³ free volume reduction confirms dense SEI formation
3.6% discharge capacity loss attributed to cathode SEI resistance during intercalation
charge capacity and OCP unaffected, indicating anode de-intercalation not impeded
post-penetration stability (~100 °C, no smoke/explosion) is evidence-bounded to additive-protected battery under tested conditions
causal link between Stage 2 SEI and Stage 3 stability is not directly established by evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming additive prevents thermal runaway universally across all nail speeds or SOC levels
Attributing Stage 3 stability causally to Stage 2 SEI formation without supporting evidence
Inferring no-additive battery behavior in Stage 3 from the additive battery data
Claiming free volume reduction alone localizes SEI to cathode

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-stage mechanistic reasoning linking additive-induced SEI formation to electrochemical and thermal observations, with explicit admissibility boundaries.
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence covers 1 mm/s penetration, 1.5 wt% additive, full-cell 1 C discharge; no cross-condition comparisons are supported.
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution attributing capacity loss to anode rather than cathode SEI

solver_view.stem: A LiCoO2 pouch cell aged under high-temperature float charge (HTFC) conditions reaches SOH = 80%. Accelerating rate calorimetry shows onset self-heating temperature (Tonset), internal-short-circuit temperature (TISC), and thermal runaway temperature (TTR) all shift relative to the fresh cell. Which of the following statements about these shifts are correct?
options: A. Tonset increases by approximately 25.5°C, indicating the cell is harder to trigger into self-heating at low temperatures.
B. TISC increases relative to the fresh cell, reflecting improved separator integrity after aging.
C. TTR decreases by approximately 12.6°C, indicating thermal runaway is triggered at a lower temperature in the aged cell.
D. The time interval between TISC and TTR shortens with aging, giving less warning time before thermal runaway.
E. TISC decreases by approximately 25.1°C, consistent with separator aging and cathode degradation raising internal short-circuit risk above 135°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states Tonset increases by 25.5°C at SOH 80%, consistent with harder-to-trigger self-heating below 135°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TISC decreases, not increases; separator aging degrades, raising short-circuit risk above threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states TTR decreases by 12.6°C at SOH 80%, confirming lower thermal runaway threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states ΔtISC increases with aging, not shortens; aged cells sustain ISC longer before TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states TISC decreases by 25.1°C at SOH 80%, linked to separator aging and cathode degradation above 135°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Direction and magnitude of thermal parameter shifts with HTFC aging at SOH 80%
analysis.primary_boundary: SOH 80% HTFC-aged LiCoO2 pouch cell; shifts relative to fresh cell only
intended_error_modes: direction reversal for TISC and ΔtISC
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing these shifts to other chemistries or aging modes
Assuming ΔtISC shortens for all aged cells

solver_view.stem: For HTFC-aged LiCoO2 cells, kinetic analysis of Stage I (Tonset to 135°C) shows that both activation energy Ea and frequency factor lgA increase linearly as SOH decreases, while reaction enthalpy H decreases linearly with decreasing SOH. Which conclusions are supported by this evidence?
options: A. As the cell ages, Stage I exothermic reactions become harder to initiate but proceed faster once triggered.
B. The net energy released during Stage I self-heating grows with aging due to the higher reaction rate.
C. Reduced H with aging reflects lower stored energy capacity, not a change in reaction pathway.
D. Higher Ea alone is sufficient to conclude the aged cell is safer than the fresh cell across all temperature ranges.
E. The simultaneous rise in lgA partially offsets the safety benefit of higher Ea by accelerating reaction rate once initiated.
F. Below 135°C, overall thermal stability improves with aging primarily because SEI decomposition and regeneration dominate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states Stage I reactions become harder to trigger but faster once initiated as SOH decreases.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: H decreases linearly with aging; total energy released falls despite higher reaction rate once triggered.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes declining H to reduced energy storage capacity with aging, not a pathway change.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Above 135°C, thermal stability decreases with aging; higher Ea applies only to Stage I, not all temperature ranges.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Rising lgA with aging means faster reaction rate once initiated, partially countering the benefit of higher Ea.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states below 135°C, thermal stability increases with decreasing SOH primarily due to SEI decomposition and regeneration.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-parameter kinetic interpretation and boundary-limited safety conclusions for Stage I
analysis.primary_boundary: Stage I only (Tonset to 135°C); HTFC aging; LiCoO2 chemistry
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim linking rate increase to total energy increase
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending Stage I kinetic conclusions to Stage II or full TR
Applying to non-HTFC aging modes

solver_view.stem: Post-mortem analysis of HTFC-aged LiCoO2 pouch cells finds significant cobalt (Co) on the anode surface. Incremental capacity (IC) analysis shows peaks associated with anode reactions decline and disappear while cathode-associated peaks stabilize. Which statements correctly interpret these findings regarding degradation mechanisms and their consequences?
options: A. Co on the anode surface indicates cathode disproportionation causing Co dissolution and migration during HTFC aging.
B. Stabilization of cathode IC peaks confirms cathode degradation is the dominant driver of capacity fade under HTFC conditions.
C. Anode degradation is the dominant contributor to capacity fade, as evidenced by disappearance of anode-associated IC peaks.
D. Co deposition on the anode is a consequence of anode degradation products migrating to the cathode surface.
E. Cathode structural changes, evidenced by Co dissolution, contribute to above-135°C thermal instability in aged cells despite not dominating capacity fade.
F. The disappearance of anode IC peaks implies complete lithium plating has occurred on the anode.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: EDS evidence directly links Co on anode to cathode disproportionation and Co dissolution/migration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Stabilization of cathode peaks means cathode is NOT the dominant capacity fade driver; anode degradation dominates.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states anode IC peaks decline and disappear while cathode peaks stabilize, confirming anode-dominant capacity fade.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Co migration direction is cathode-to-anode, not anode-to-cathode; mechanism attribution is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cathode degradation raises above-135°C thermal instability per C003, even though anode degradation dominates capacity fade.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes IC peak disappearance to anode degradation generally; lithium plating is not stated as the mechanism.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing capacity fade mechanism from thermal instability mechanism in HTFC aging
analysis.primary_boundary: HTFC-aged LiCoO2 pouch cells; IC and EDS evidence scope only
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution for Co migration direction
conflating capacity fade dominance with thermal degradation dominance
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing anode-dominant fade to other aging protocols
Inferring complete lithium plating from IC peak disappearance

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer is evaluating HTFC-aged LiCoO2 pouch cells at two SOH levels (90% and 70%) for use in a module that may experience temperatures ranging from 80°C to 160°C during fault conditions. The engineer notes that gas venting during aging produces a mixed gas with a lower flammability limit (LFL) of approximately 27.94 vol%. Explain why a single thermal stability ranking (e.g., 'lower SOH = safer') cannot be applied across this full temperature range, and identify which degradation mechanisms drive the reversal in stability ranking at the critical threshold.

ground_truth.gold_answer: A single ranking cannot apply because a critical threshold at approximately 135°C separates two opposite stability regimes. Below 135°C (within the 80–135°C portion of the fault range), lower SOH cells are more thermally stable: SEI decomposition and regeneration during aging reduce reactivity, raising Tonset and making self-heating harder to trigger. Above 135°C (135–160°C portion), lower SOH cells are less stable: separator aging reduces mechanical integrity, increasing internal short-circuit risk (TISC decreases), and cathode degradation including Co dissolution raises thermal runaway susceptibility (TTR decreases). Therefore the 70% SOH cell is relatively safer below 135°C but more dangerous above it compared to the 90% SOH cell. The LFL of ~27.94 vol% indicates vented gases are flammable but not easily ignitable, representing a separate, relatively lower explosion hazard independent of the stability reversal.
must_include: 135°C critical threshold separating two opposite stability patterns
Below 135°C: lower SOH improves stability via SEI decomposition and regeneration
Above 135°C: lower SOH worsens stability due to separator aging and cathode degradation
TISC and TTR decrease with aging above the threshold
LFL ~27.94 vol% indicates flammable but not easily ignitable gases
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming lower SOH is universally safer or universally more dangerous
Applying this reversal pattern to aging modes other than HTFC
Asserting the gas mixture poses negligible risk without referencing the LFL boundary

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary-conditioned thermal stability ranking reversal and its mechanistic drivers across a fault temperature range
analysis.primary_boundary: 135°C threshold; HTFC-aged LiCoO2 pouch cells only; fault range 80–160°C spans both regimes
intended_error_modes: unsupported monotonicity (lower SOH always safer/always more dangerous)
boundary overreach applying below-135°C conclusion to full range
omitting LFL context for gas hazard

solver_view.stem: Two aged lithium-ion battery groups are overcharge-tested to thermal runaway: one cycled at 0°C (LTC) and one at 55°C (HTC), both at 80% SOH. During the overcharge period from lithium-plating onset to current cutoff, LTC batteries show ~72°C temperature rise versus ~38°C for HTC. Which statements correctly explain this difference?
options: A LTC batteries accumulate deposited lithium that reacts exothermically with electrolyte, contributing >50% of side-reaction heat and driving the larger temperature rise.
B HTC batteries have thickened SEI films that hinder lithium-ion transport, suppressing lithium-electrolyte reaction rates and reducing their side-reaction heat contribution to <30%.
C Large-scale internal short-circuiting dominates the temperature difference, contributing substantially more heat in LTC than HTC batteries.
D HTC batteries generate more total heat than LTC batteries during overcharge because higher SEI resistance increases ohmic dissipation.
E The temperature difference arises because LTC batteries reach lithium-plating onset voltage earlier, before any electrochemical side reactions begin.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LTC aging deposits lithium; its electrolyte reaction exceeds 50% of side-reaction heat, explaining the larger temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI thickening in HTC suppresses lithium transport and reaction rate, reducing contribution below 30% and lowering temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Internal short-circuit heat is ~60% across all aging paths with minimal variation; it cannot explain the differential temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows HTC batteries have lower peak TR temperature and smaller temperature rise, contradicting greater total heat generation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lithium plating onset requires anode potential below 0 V vs. Li; side reactions begin before plating onset, not after, making this sequence wrong.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism linking aging-path-dependent lithium deposition and SEI state to differential overcharge heat generation
analysis.primary_boundary: Side-reaction heat fractions are specific to LTC (>50%) and HTC (<30%) aging paths; internal short-circuit heat is path-invariant
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing short-circuit heat dominance to explain differential temperature rise
Claiming HTC generates more total heat due to resistance increase

solver_view.stem: A researcher models deposited-lithium concentration for low-temperature cycled (LTC) batteries using cLi,0 = 1 − SOH, assuming capacity loss arises entirely from lithium plating.
